questions.stem: A researcher studying the origin of blood cancers explains that leukemias, lymphomas, and multiple myeloma all trace back to a single population of cells that also produces normal red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets. From which of the following cells do hematopoietic neoplasms arise?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hematopoietic stem cells in the bone marrow

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mature plasma cells circulating in the blood

choices.label: D
choices.text: Endothelial cells lining the lymphatic vessels

choices.label: E
choices.text: Epithelial stem cells of the thymic cortex

explanation.summary: Hematopoietic neoplasms arise from the primordial hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) of the bone marrow.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hematopoietic neoplasms arise from the primordial hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) found in the bone marrow. These same stem cells also give rise to normal blood cells such as red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets, but under influences like genetic mutations, toxins, radiation, and medications they can develop into neoplasms.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Reticuloendothelial cells of the spleen are not the source of hematopoietic neoplasms, which originate from marrow stem cells.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mature plasma cells are a downstream derivative of the lymphoid line; the neoplasms as a group arise from the upstream hematopoietic stem cell.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lymphatic endothelial cells are structural vessel cells and do not give rise to hematopoietic neoplasms.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thymic epithelial stem cells give rise to the thymic stroma, not to the hematopoietic neoplasms described here.

questions.stem: A pathologist is applying the current World Health Organization (WHO) classification to a newly diagnosed hematopoietic neoplasm. According to this system, which of the following is used as the main framework for categorizing these tumors?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The cell of origin, either myeloid or lymphoid

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether the patient is a child or an adult

choices.label: C
choices.text: The presence or absence of lymphadenopathy

choices.label: D
choices.text: The response of the tumor to chemotherapy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Whether the disease is acute or chronic

explanation.summary: The WHO classification uses the cell of origin (myeloid vs lymphoid) as the primary framework.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick emphasizes the current WHO classification, which uses the cell of origin as the main framework, dividing hematopoietic neoplasms into those derived from the myeloid cell line and those derived from the lymphoid cell line.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Age is a helpful clinical clue (myeloid neoplasms are more common in adults) but is not the main WHO framework.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Lymphadenopathy helps distinguish lymphomas from leukemias clinically but is not the organizing principle of the WHO system.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Treatment response is not the basis of the WHO classification.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The acute versus chronic distinction applies within leukemias but is not the primary myeloid-versus-lymphoid framework.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man is being evaluated for a hematopoietic malignancy. His physician notes that distinguishing a leukemia from a lymphoma can be difficult but that certain features help. Which of the following findings is most characteristic of a leukemia rather than a lymphoma?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alterations in peripheral blood cell counts

choices.label: E
choices.text: Origin restricted to the lymphoid cell line

explanation.summary: Because of their bone marrow origin, leukemias typically alter blood cell counts, whereas lymphomas typically cause lymphadenopathy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Due to their bone marrow origin, leukemias typically have alterations in blood cell counts, which is less common in lymphomas (although it can occur in lymphomas that involve the bone). Lymphomas, by contrast, typically present with lymphadenopathy.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Lymphadenopathy is characteristic of lymphomas, not leukemias.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Weight loss is a generalized symptom that can be seen in both leukemias and lymphomas.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fever is a nonspecific symptom seen in both leukemias and lymphomas.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Only lymphomas are restricted to the lymphoid line; leukemias can arise from either the myeloid or lymphoid cell line.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman is diagnosed with an acute leukemia. Compared with a chronic leukemia, which of the following best characterizes the cells responsible for her disease and its expected course if untreated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Very immature precursor cells with short survival of weeks to months

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mature, well-differentiated cells with survival measured in years

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cells arrested at a single intermediate stage with an indolent course

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fully mature lymphocytes confined to peripheral lymph nodes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Dysplastic precursors that never enter the peripheral blood

explanation.summary: Acute leukemias are caused by very immature precursor cells and, if untreated, carry short survival of weeks to months.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acute leukemias are caused by very immature precursor hematopoietic cells and have a more abrupt, rapid presentation. Because of the rapid proliferation that takes over the bone marrow, patients exhibit short survival (weeks to months) if not treated. Chronic leukemias, by contrast, are caused by more mature cells at an advanced stage of development and have longer survival (years).

whyWrongByChoice.B: Mature cells with survival in years describe chronic, not acute, leukemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: An indolent course reflects chronic disease; acute leukemia is rapidly progressive.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Confinement of mature lymphocytes to lymph nodes describes a lymphoma pattern, not acute leukemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dysplastic precursors that fail to mature describe myelodysplastic neoplasms rather than the immature precursors of acute leukemia.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man is found to have a myeloproliferative neoplasm in which the marrow shows a striking overproduction of red blood cells and his hematocrit is markedly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Among myeloproliferative neoplasms, red blood cell dominance defines polycythemia vera.
explanation.whyCorrect: In myeloproliferative neoplasms, the disorders are separated based on which cell line becomes dominant. Polycythemia vera is characterized by red blood cell (RBC) dominance, matching the overproduction of red cells and elevated hematocrit described here.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Essential thrombocythemia is defined by platelet dominance, not red cell overproduction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chronic myeloid leukemia is characterized by granulocyte dominance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Primary myelofibrosis features expansion of all precursor lines with eventual marrow fibrosis rather than isolated red cell dominance.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Myelodysplastic neoplasm causes marrow failure with low blood counts, not an overproduction of red cells.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man has a myeloid neoplasm in which the bone marrow is replaced by abnormal, dysplastic precursor cells that have not differentiated into mature blood cells, resulting in low peripheral blood counts. Approximately what proportion of patients with this disorder progress to acute myeloid leukemia?

explanation.summary: In myelodysplastic neoplasms, transformation to AML occurs in about 30% of patients.
explanation.whyCorrect: In myelodysplastic neoplasms, the marrow is replaced by dysplastic cells derived from progenitor myeloid stem cells that have not differentiated into mature blood cells, causing bone marrow failure with low counts. Transformation to AML occurs in 30% of patients.

whyWrongByChoice.B: 5% understates the risk; the brick specifies transformation in 30% of patients.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 50% is higher than the figure given for progression to AML.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 75% overstates the risk of transformation in myelodysplastic neoplasms.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Not all patients progress; the stated proportion is 30%, not 100%.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman presents with fatigue and recurrent infections. Her bone marrow is filled with malignant myeloid cells, and a complete blood count shows pancytopenia. This represents a true malignant transformation of myeloid cells. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: AML is a true malignant transformation of myeloid cells that fills the marrow, causing pancytopenia and frequent infections.
explanation.whyCorrect: In acute myeloid leukemia (AML), there is true malignant transformation of myeloid cells. The malignant cells fill the bone marrow and can extend locally or metastasize, often causing bone marrow failure and pancytopenia. Infections are common because of the reduced number and function of circulating white blood cells.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Polycythemia vera is a myeloproliferative neoplasm with red cell overproduction, not pancytopenia from malignant transformation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Essential thrombocythemia shows platelet dominance rather than marrow-filling malignant transformation with pancytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Follicular lymphoma is a mature B-cell (lymphoid) neoplasm, not a myeloid malignancy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Multiple myeloma is a plasma cell neoplasm of lymphoid origin, distinct from a malignant transformation of myeloid cells.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy is diagnosed with a precursor T-cell neoplasm. His disease predominantly involves the blood and bone marrow, so it is called T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (T-ALL); a closely related presentation predominantly involving tissue such as lymph nodes and thymus is called T-lymphoblastic lymphoma (T-LBL). Which of the following best describes the relationship between T-ALL and T-LBL?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are the same disease, distinguished mainly by the predominant site of involvement

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are unrelated diseases that share only a name

choices.label: C
choices.text: T-ALL is a myeloid neoplasm and T-LBL is a lymphoid neoplasm

choices.label: D
choices.text: T-LBL is a mature neoplasm while T-ALL is a plasma cell neoplasm

choices.label: E
choices.text: They differ because one expresses B-cell markers and the other expresses T-cell markers

explanation.summary: T-ALL and T-LBL are the same precursor T-cell disease, separated by whether the blood/marrow or tissue is predominantly involved.
explanation.whyCorrect: A precursor T-cell neoplasm that occurs predominantly in the blood and bone marrow is called T-ALL, whereas one predominantly in tissue (lymph nodes, thymus) is called T-LBL. They are the same disease, and because the overall site of involvement may not be known at presentation, the neoplasm is usually named T-lymphoblastic leukemia/lymphoma.

whyWrongByChoice.B: They are not unrelated; the brick states explicitly that they are the same disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Both are precursor T-cell (lymphoid) neoplasms; neither is myeloid.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Both are precursor neoplasms, and neither is a plasma cell neoplasm.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Both arise from T cells; the distinction is site of involvement, not B-cell versus T-cell marker expression.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man has a years-long history of slowly progressive skin patches that have evolved into skin tumors and a near-full-body desquamating rash. Biopsy shows a mature T-cell neoplasm. He is told there is no cure but that his life expectancy is generally normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Mycosis fungoides/Sezary syndrome is a mature T-cell neoplasm with slow-growing skin lesions and generally normal life expectancy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mycosis fungoides/Sezary syndrome is the emphasized mature peripheral T-cell neoplasm. Patients have slow-growing skin lesions that can later develop into skin tumors or a near-full-body desquamating rash. There is no cure, but because it is slow-growing, patients generally have a normal life expectancy.

whyWrongByChoice.B: T-ALL/LBL is a precursor T-cell neoplasm seen mostly in children, not an indolent adult skin disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Burkitt lymphoma is a high-grade B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma that grows explosively, not a chronic skin disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hodgkin lymphoma is defined by Reed-Sternberg cells and presents with lymphadenopathy, not desquamating skin lesions.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Multiple myeloma is a plasma cell neoplasm affecting marrow, bone, and kidney, not the skin.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman presents with bone pain, anemia, recurrent infections, and acute kidney injury. Workup reveals massive proliferation of plasma cells in the bone marrow, precipitation of immunoglobulins in the kidney, and an elevated serum calcium. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Multiple myeloma is a plasma cell neoplasm causing marrow failure, immunoglobulin-related kidney failure, bone lesions, and hypercalcemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Plasma cell neoplasms derive from plasma cells, the B-cell derivative responsible for antibody secretion. The most common is multiple myeloma, with massive proliferation of plasma cells in the bone marrow. Consequences include bone marrow failure (infections, anemia), precipitation of immunoglobulins in the kidney causing kidney failure, and bone lesions with fractures and secondary hypercalcemia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia is a mature B-cell neoplasm that does not classically cause immunoglobulin precipitation in the kidney and hypercalcemia from bone destruction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hodgkin lymphoma is defined by Reed-Sternberg cells and lymphadenopathy, not plasma cell proliferation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Acute myeloid leukemia is a myeloid malignancy filling the marrow, not a plasma cell neoplasm.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mycosis fungoides/Sezary syndrome is a mature T-cell skin neoplasm, unrelated to this plasma cell picture.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents with painless cervical lymphadenopathy, fevers, and night sweats. A lymph node biopsy shows gigantic cells with at least two large nuclei, each containing a single nucleolus, producing an "owl's-eye" appearance. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Hodgkin lymphoma is defined by neoplastic Reed-Sternberg cells, which are gigantic and have an owl's-eye appearance.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hodgkin lymphoma usually occurs in young adults ages 15 to 34 years and is defined by the presence of neoplastic Reed-Sternberg cells. These cells have a distinct morphology: they are gigantic and often have at least two big nuclei with single nucleoli, giving the cell an owl's-eye appearance.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma is a high-grade non-Hodgkin lymphoma and is not defined by Reed-Sternberg cells.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Follicular lymphoma is a low-grade non-Hodgkin lymphoma without owl's-eye Reed-Sternberg cells.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Multiple myeloma involves plasma cells in the marrow, not Reed-Sternberg cells in a lymph node.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chronic myeloid leukemia is a myeloproliferative neoplasm with granulocyte dominance, not a Reed-Sternberg cell lymphoma.

questions.stem: A young patient presents to the emergency department with fatigue and bone pain. An initial complete blood count identifies an abundance of blasts, confirming a diagnosis of acute leukemia. Which of the following laboratory methods would be most useful to confirm the lineage (cell type/origin) of the leukemia?

explanation.summary: Flow cytometry classifies leukemia lineage by reading surface antigens on the blasts, distinguishing lymphoid (ALL) from myeloid (AML).
explanation.whyCorrect: By analyzing the antigens on the cell surface of the blasts, flow cytometry can confirm the lineage (cell type/origin) of the leukemia. The way a leukemia is identified as lymphoid (ALL) or myeloid (AML) is primarily by flow cytometry, and it works well on peripheral blood.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Conventional cytogenetics detects abnormalities in chromosome number and structure, not the surface-antigen lineage of the cells.
whyWrongByChoice.C: FISH evaluates select chromosomes with specific probes and does not establish cell lineage.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Molecular analysis detects specific mutations of the leukemia but not the lineage of the cells.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Routine histology requires tissue and cannot be performed on peripheral blood, so it is not the method to confirm lineage here.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old man with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma completes two cycles of combination chemotherapy. Imaging shows a dramatic reduction in tumor size, but disease is still detectable. He asks why he cannot simply stop, since the chemotherapy is clearly working. Which principle best explains why several additional cycles are still required?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Each dose eliminates a fixed number of tumor cells regardless of tumor size

choices.label: B
choices.text: Each dose kills a constant proportion of the remaining tumor cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: The tumor has already developed complete resistance to the regimen

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chemotherapy only becomes effective after the immune system is fully suppressed

choices.label: E
choices.text: A single adequate dose eradicates all malignant cells if given at maximum tolerated dose

explanation.summary: The log-cell kill hypothesis states chemotherapy kills a constant fraction, not a constant number, of cancer cells per dose.
explanation.whyCorrect: By the log-cell kill hypothesis, a given dose kills a constant proportion of tumor cells, reflecting first-order kinetics. Because each cycle removes only a fraction (e.g., 90-95%) of the cells that remain, a few cells persist after every dose, so multiple cycles are needed to drive the burden down to a clinically insignificant level.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The hypothesis specifically states a constant proportion, not a constant number, is killed per dose.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Persistent detectable disease after a good response reflects fractional killing kinetics, not established resistance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cytotoxic chemotherapy does not require immunosuppression to work; that is not the mechanism described.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The article emphasizes that a single treatment rarely eradicates all cells, which is why cycles are required.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with a large but resectable breast tumor is given chemotherapy before her planned mastectomy in order to shrink the primary mass and address distant micro-metastases, making surgery easier. This treatment strategy is best described as which of the following?

explanation.summary: Neoadjuvant therapy is given before the primary treatment to shrink the tumor and micro-metastases.
explanation.whyCorrect: Neoadjuvant therapy is administered before the primary treatment (such as surgery) to shrink both the primary cancer, making it easier to resect, and distant micro-metastases. This matches the timing and intent described in the vignette.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Induction therapy is the first-line treatment aimed at killing as many cancer cells as possible, not specifically pre-surgical shrinkage.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Adjuvant therapy is given after the primary treatment to improve outcomes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Salvage therapy is used only after preferred therapies have failed to produce remission.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Maintenance therapy is not the strategy described; the timing here is clearly before surgery.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man undergoes complete surgical resection of a colon cancer. To reduce the risk of recurrence from residual microscopic disease, he then receives a course of chemotherapy. This post-operative chemotherapy is best classified as which of the following?

explanation.summary: Adjuvant therapy is given after the primary treatment to improve outcomes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Adjuvant therapy is defined as treatment given after the primary treatment to improve outcomes. Chemotherapy given after surgical resection to eliminate residual microscopic disease fits this definition.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Neoadjuvant therapy is given before, not after, the primary treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Induction therapy is the first-line treatment to kill as many cancer cells as possible, not post-operative therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Salvage therapy is reserved for cases where preferred therapies have already failed.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Endocrine therapy is a mechanism-based class for hormone-responsive cancers, not a timing category.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man with Hodgkin lymphoma has relapsed after first-line and second-line regimens, both of which failed to produce remission. His oncologist now selects a chemotherapy regimen used specifically because all preferred therapies have been exhausted. This regimen is best described as which of the following?

explanation.summary: Salvage therapy is used when all preferred therapies have been tried and failed to produce remission.
explanation.whyCorrect: Salvage therapy is defined as treatment used when all preferred therapies have been tried and have failed to produce cancer remission. The refractory, multiply-relapsed scenario matches this definition.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Induction is the initial, first-line treatment, not the option chosen after failures.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Neoadjuvant therapy precedes a primary treatment such as surgery.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Adjuvant therapy follows a primary treatment to improve outcomes, not after multiple failures.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Consolidation therapy is not the category defined by the article for this refractory setting.

questions.stem: A patient's tumor initially responds to chemotherapy but later progresses. Laboratory analysis of the resistant tumor cells shows that they now rapidly extrude the drug via an upregulated membrane transporter, an effect promoted by tumor-derived interleukin-6. Which of the following resistance mechanisms is most consistent with these findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased efflux through the multiple drug-resistance transporter

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased drug uptake from loss of an inward transport protein

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased intracellular glutathione that inactivates the drug

choices.label: E
choices.text: Altered DNA methylation reducing drug sensitivity

explanation.summary: The MDR transporter pumps drug out of the cell and can be induced by cytokines such as IL-6.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cancer cells may pump the drug out as fast as it enters using the multiple drug-resistance (MDR) protein, a membrane transporter that shuttles drugs out of the cell. This efflux can arise from a direct mutation or be induced by cytokines like interleukin-6 produced by the tumor cells, matching the findings described.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Decreased uptake involves failure of the inward transport protein, not active efflux driven by IL-6.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DNA-repair enzyme upregulation corrects drug-induced breaks; it does not extrude the drug.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glutathione reacts with and inactivates the drug intracellularly rather than pumping it out.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Altered methylation changes gene expression and sensitivity but is not a membrane efflux mechanism.

questions.stem: An oncologist explains to a trainee that a single cytotoxic drug often fails because the original tumor is not a uniform population; it contains many genetic variants, and the subset that happens to resist the drug survives and proliferates. Combination chemotherapy is used to counter this problem. Which concept does this scenario best illustrate?

explanation.summary: Tumors contain many genetic variants, and pre-existing resistant subclones can survive and expand.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cancers are not usually a uniform population but harbor many genetic variants, some of which resist a given drug and proliferate. This heterogeneity is a key reason combination chemotherapy is preferred, since a second drug may kill cells resistant to the first.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Log-cell kill describes the fractional killing per dose, not the genetic diversity of the tumor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Progression-free survival is an outcome measure, not a mechanism of resistance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Myelosuppression is an adverse effect, unrelated to why monotherapy fails.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Neoadjuvant sequencing refers to timing of therapy, not tumor cell diversity.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman receives an anthracycline as part of her chemotherapy regimen. Her care team schedules serial cardiac function monitoring because this agent is best known for which of the following characteristic adverse effects?

explanation.summary: Doxorubicin (an anthracycline) is classically associated with cardiotoxicity.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ChemoMan adverse-effect map links doxorubicin (anthracyclines) with cardiotoxicity, which is why cardiac monitoring is warranted with these agents.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Pulmonary fibrosis is characteristic of bleomycin and busulfan, not anthracyclines.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hemorrhagic cystitis is associated with cyclophosphamide.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Peripheral neuropathy is associated with the vinca alkaloids.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ototoxicity is linked to cisplatin and carboplatin.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old man treated for testicular cancer develops progressive dyspnea and a dry cough. Pulmonary function testing shows a restrictive pattern with reduced diffusing capacity, and imaging suggests interstitial fibrosis. Which chemotherapeutic agent in his regimen most likely caused this finding?

explanation.summary: Bleomycin characteristically causes pulmonary fibrosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ChemoMan map associates bleomycin (along with busulfan) with pulmonary fibrosis, which fits the restrictive lung disease and interstitial fibrosis described.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Doxorubicin is associated with cardiotoxicity, not pulmonary fibrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Vincristine causes peripheral neuropathy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cyclophosphamide is linked to hemorrhagic cystitis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cytarabine is associated with keratitis and conjunctivitis.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man receiving cisplatin-based chemotherapy develops a rising serum creatinine along with new high-frequency hearing loss and tinnitus. These adverse effects are most characteristic of which drug class?

explanation.summary: Platinum agents such as cisplatin and carboplatin cause nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ChemoMan map links cisplatin/carboplatin (platinum agents) to both nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity, matching the rising creatinine and hearing loss with tinnitus described.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Anthracyclines such as doxorubicin cause cardiotoxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Vinca alkaloids cause peripheral neuropathy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Antimetabolites such as cytarabine cause keratitis/conjunctivitis and methotrexate causes myelosuppression.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Alkylating agents such as cyclophosphamide cause hemorrhagic cystitis.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman receiving cyclophosphamide develops gross hematuria and suprapubic pain, and cystoscopy shows diffuse bladder mucosal inflammation and bleeding. This adverse effect is most characteristic of which agent?

explanation.summary: Cyclophosphamide is classically associated with hemorrhagic cystitis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ChemoMan map links cyclophosphamide to hemorrhagic cystitis, which matches the hematuria, suprapubic pain, and inflamed bleeding bladder mucosa described.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Methotrexate is associated with myelosuppression.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Doxorubicin is associated with cardiotoxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bleomycin is associated with pulmonary fibrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Irinotecan is associated with severe acute and delayed diarrhea.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man being treated with a vinca alkaloid develops distal numbness, tingling, and diminished ankle reflexes. Consistent with this agent's mechanism, vinca alkaloids exert their cytotoxic effect primarily by acting on which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Microtubules of the mitotic spindle during M phase

choices.label: D
choices.text: DNA cross-linking independent of the cell cycle

explanation.summary: Vinca alkaloids are microtubule inhibitors that act during mitosis and cause peripheral neuropathy.
explanation.whyCorrect: The cell-cycle diagram places vinca alkaloids (with taxanes) among microtubule inhibitors that act at mitosis (M phase), interfering with the spindle. Clinically, vinca alkaloids are also linked to peripheral neuropathy, matching the sensory symptoms described.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Targeting S-phase nucleotide synthesis is the mechanism of antimetabolites such as cytarabine and methotrexate.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Topoisomerase inhibition is the mechanism of irinotecan.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cell cycle-independent DNA cross-linking describes alkylating and platinum agents.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Estrogen receptor signaling is the target of endocrine therapy such as tamoxifen.

questions.stem: A 61-year-old woman with an estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer is started on tamoxifen. This drug is an example of which category of cancer therapy?

explanation.summary: Endocrine therapy treats hormone-responsive cancers by blocking or modulating hormones; tamoxifen is the example for ER-positive breast cancer.
explanation.whyCorrect: Endocrine therapy treats specific hormone-responsive cancers by blocking or modulating certain hormones. The article gives tamoxifen used for estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer as the prototypical example.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cytotoxic chemotherapy uses cellular poisons to prevent growth and replication; methotrexate is its example.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Molecularly targeted therapy hits specific receptors/pathways, exemplified by imatinib in CML.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Immunotherapy helps the immune system attack cancer, exemplified by pembrolizumab.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Alkylating chemotherapy is a cytotoxic subtype that cross-links DNA, not a hormone-modulating therapy.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old girl is brought to the office with 3 weeks of fatigue, decreased activity, and easy bruising. Examination shows pallor and scattered petechiae. A complete blood count reveals an elevated white blood cell count, low hemoglobin, and a very low platelet count. A peripheral blood smear shows numerous lymphoblasts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: A child with cytopenias and circulating lymphoblasts has acute lymphoblastic leukemia/lymphoma until proven otherwise.
explanation.whyCorrect: In ALL, malignant lymphoblasts proliferate in and replace the marrow, crowding out normal precursors and producing anemia and thrombocytopenia while lymphoblasts spill into the blood. ALL is the most common childhood malignancy, so a child presenting with an acute leukemia is assumed to have ALL until proven otherwise.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Acute myeloid leukemia produces myeloblasts (often with Auer rods) rather than lymphoblasts and is far less common in children.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura causes isolated thrombocytopenia without anemia or circulating blasts.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aplastic anemia causes pancytopenia with a hypocellular marrow and does not produce circulating blasts.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Infectious mononucleosis produces reactive atypical lymphocytes, which are mature cells, not lymphoblasts.

questions.stem: A pediatrician explains that a child who presents with an acute leukemia should be assumed to have a particular disease until proven otherwise, and that this disease is also the most common malignancy of childhood, accounting for about 25% of childhood cancers. Which of the following is this disease?

explanation.summary: ALL is the most common malignancy of childhood and the default assumption for a child with acute leukemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: ALL is much more common in children than adults and is the most common childhood malignancy overall (about 40 cases per million children), accounting for roughly 25% of childhood cancers. In children it also carries a good prognosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Acute myeloid leukemia occurs in children but is much less common than ALL.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hodgkin lymphoma typically affects older adolescents and adults and is not the most common childhood malignancy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chronic myeloid leukemia is largely an adult disease and is rare in children.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Neuroblastoma is a common childhood solid tumor but is not a leukemia and is not the most common childhood malignancy overall.

questions.stem: A 15-year-old boy presents with cough, dyspnea, and facial swelling. A chest radiograph reveals a large anterior mediastinal mass. A complete blood count shows lymphoblasts, and biopsy confirms a lymphoblastic malignancy whose cells express CD2, CD3, and CD7. Which of the following is the most likely subtype?

explanation.summary: T-cell ALL commonly presents as an anterior mediastinal mass and expresses T-cell markers such as CD2, CD3, and CD7.
explanation.whyCorrect: T-cell ALL frequently presents with lymph node or mediastinal involvement, and an anterior mediastinal mass can cause cardiorespiratory compromise. T-cell ALL is usually positive for CD2, CD3, CD4, CD7, and CD8, matching this immunophenotype.

whyWrongByChoice.B: B-cell ALL is usually positive for CD19, CD22, and CD79a and is less associated with a mediastinal mass.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hodgkin lymphoma is characterized by Reed-Sternberg cells, not lymphoblasts expressing TdT and T-cell CD markers.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Follicular lymphoma is a mature B-cell lymphoma of adults, not a lymphoblastic malignancy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chronic myeloid leukemia is a myeloid disorder and does not present as a lymphoblastic mediastinal mass.

questions.stem: A pathologist reviews marrow blasts from a newly diagnosed acute leukemia. The cells show fine chromatin, prominent nucleoli, and scant bluish cytoplasm, and no Auer rods are identified. Absence of Auer rods is most useful in distinguishing this leukemia from which of the following?

explanation.summary: Auer rods are seen in myeloblasts of acute myeloid leukemia and myelodysplastic syndrome but not in the lymphoblasts of ALL.
explanation.whyCorrect: Unlike the myeloblasts of acute myeloid leukemia and myelodysplastic syndrome, ALL lymphoblasts do not contain Auer rods (crystalline cytoplasmic inclusions). Their absence therefore helps distinguish ALL from a myeloid leukemia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura is not a blastic malignancy, so Auer rods are not part of its differentiation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Auer rods do not distinguish B-cell from T-cell ALL because lymphoblasts of both subtypes lack them.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Follicular lymphoma is a mature B-cell lymphoma and is not evaluated by the presence of Auer rods.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Multiple myeloma is a plasma cell neoplasm and does not present with Auer rod-containing blasts.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with newly diagnosed acute lymphoblastic leukemia is found on cytogenetic testing to have the t(9;22) translocation (Philadelphia chromosome). Addition of which of the following agents to his chemotherapy regimen is most appropriate?

explanation.summary: Philadelphia chromosome-positive ALL is treated with the addition of a tyrosine kinase inhibitor such as dasatinib.
explanation.whyCorrect: The t(9;22) translocation produces the BCR-ABL fusion gene and its constitutively active tyrosine kinase. In Ph-positive ALL a tyrosine kinase inhibitor is added, and long-term dasatinib-based therapy has shown better outcomes than imatinib.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Vincristine is a standard component of induction but is not targeted to the BCR-ABL kinase.
whyWrongByChoice.C: L-asparaginase is a standard chemotherapeutic and has no relation to the Philadelphia chromosome.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Rasburicase is used to treat hyperuricemia in tumor lysis syndrome, not to target BCR-ABL.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Prednisone is a glucocorticoid backbone agent and does not specifically target the t(9;22) kinase.

questions.stem: A child with acute lymphoblastic leukemia and a high white blood cell count is about to begin induction chemotherapy. To prevent uric acid precipitation in the renal tubules and resulting acute renal failure, which of the following is the most appropriate prophylaxis?

explanation.summary: IV hydration and allopurinol are given to prevent tumor lysis syndrome and its uric acid nephropathy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tumor lysis syndrome releases potassium, phosphate, and nucleic acids that are catabolized to uric acid, which can precipitate in the renal tubules and cause acute renal failure. IV hydration and allopurinol, which blocks uric acid production, are given to prevent this.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Platelet transfusion addresses thrombocytopenic bleeding, not uric acid nephropathy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Intrathecal methotrexate is given for CNS prophylaxis, not for tumor lysis syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Broad-spectrum antibiotics treat infection, which does not prevent uric acid precipitation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Packed red blood cells correct anemia and have no role in preventing tumor lysis syndrome.

questions.stem: A patient with acute lymphoblastic leukemia develops tumor lysis syndrome and has a markedly elevated uric acid level despite allopurinol. Rasburicase is administered. Rasburicase lowers the uric acid level by which of the following mechanisms?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blocking xanthine oxidase to reduce uric acid production

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increasing renal reabsorption of uric acid

explanation.summary: Rasburicase is a recombinant urate-oxidase enzyme that converts existing uric acid to allantoin.
explanation.whyCorrect: When the uric acid level is already elevated, rasburicase is administered. It is a recombinant urate-oxidase enzyme that converts uric acid to allantoin, an inactive and soluble metabolite, thereby lowering the existing uric acid burden.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Blocking xanthine oxidase to reduce uric acid production describes allopurinol, which prevents formation rather than degrading existing uric acid.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inhibiting purine synthesis is not the mechanism of rasburicase.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Urinary alkalinization is a separate supportive measure and is not how rasburicase acts.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Rasburicase lowers uric acid; it does not increase its renal reabsorption.

questions.stem: One agent in a pediatric ALL induction regimen is an enzyme derived from bacteria that deprives leukemic cells of an amino acid they are unable to synthesize because they lack the necessary synthetase. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of this agent?

explanation.summary: Asparaginase depletes circulating asparagine, starving leukemic cells that cannot synthesize it.
explanation.whyCorrect: Asparaginase catalyzes conversion of L-asparagine into aspartic acid and ammonia, reducing circulating asparagine. Leukemic cells lack asparagine synthetase activity and cannot make their own, so depriving them of an exogenous source leads to cell death.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Inhibiting microtubule assembly is the mechanism of vincristine, not asparaginase.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inhibiting dihydrofolate reductase is the mechanism of methotrexate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inhibiting BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase describes tyrosine kinase inhibitors used in Ph-positive ALL.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cross-linking DNA describes an alkylating agent such as cyclophosphamide.

questions.stem: A 9-year-old receiving induction chemotherapy for acute lymphoblastic leukemia develops paresthesias, loss of deep tendon reflexes, and constipation. Which chemotherapeutic agent, acting by inhibition of microtubule function, is most likely responsible for these findings?

explanation.summary: Vincristine inhibits microtubule function and causes dose-related peripheral neuropathy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Vincristine acts by inhibition of microtubule function, and its use is associated with dose-related peripheral neuropathy that can cause paresthesia, loss of reflexes, weakness, and autonomic effects including vocal cord paralysis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Daunorubicin inhibits DNA/RNA synthesis and causes cumulative cardiomyopathy, not peripheral neuropathy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: L-asparaginase causes pancreatitis, coagulopathy, and hepatic toxicity rather than neuropathy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cytarabine causes myelosuppression and megaloblastic anemia, not this neuropathic pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Prednisone causes steroid-associated effects such as fractures and mood changes, not peripheral neuropathy.

questions.stem: Prior to starting systemic therapy, a child with acute lymphoblastic leukemia undergoes a lumbar puncture through which chemotherapy is delivered into the subarachnoid space. What is the primary rationale for delivering chemotherapy by this route?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The CNS is a sanctuary site

choices.label: B
choices.text: To obtain a bone marrow sample

explanation.summary: Intrathecal chemotherapy is used because the CNS is a sanctuary site with barriers that resist systemic chemotherapy.
explanation.whyCorrect: ALL has a high propensity to disseminate to the CNS, which is concerning because the CNS has built-in barriers that make it resistant to some systemic chemotherapy. Intrathecal chemotherapy delivered into the subarachnoid space, along with prophylactic dosing, targets this sanctuary site.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A bone marrow sample is obtained by bone marrow biopsy/aspirate, not by intrathecal drug delivery.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thrombocytopenic bleeding is managed with platelet support, not intrathecal chemotherapy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hyperuricemia is managed with hydration, allopurinol, or rasburicase, not intrathecal therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mediastinal disease is evaluated with imaging such as a chest radiograph, not a lumbar puncture.

questions.stem: A teenager treated for acute lymphoblastic leukemia is monitored for cumulative dose-related cardiac toxicity during therapy. Which agent in the induction regimen is most associated with cardiomyopathy?

explanation.summary: Daunorubicin causes cumulative dose-related cardiomyopathy that can progress to heart failure.
explanation.whyCorrect: Daunorubicin inhibits DNA/RNA synthesis, and its characteristic toxicity is cumulative dose-related cardiomyopathy, which can also lead to heart failure, so cardiac function is monitored during therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Vincristine causes peripheral neuropathy, not cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: L-asparaginase causes pancreatitis, coagulopathy, and hepatic toxicity rather than cardiac toxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Methotrexate causes myelosuppression, nephrotoxicity, hepatotoxicity, and mucositis, not cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cytarabine causes myelosuppression and megaloblastic anemia rather than cardiomyopathy.

questions.stem: Flow cytometry of a child's marrow blasts shows expression of CD19, CD22, and CD79a, and the nuclear enzyme terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase (TdT) is positive. These findings best support which of the following diagnoses?

explanation.summary: CD19, CD22, and CD79a with nuclear TdT positivity identify B-cell ALL.
explanation.whyCorrect: B-cell ALL is usually positive for CD19, CD22, and CD79a. TdT is expressed in the nucleus of lymphoblasts and disappears as the cell matures, so its presence supports a lymphoblastic malignancy; together these findings indicate B-cell ALL.

whyWrongByChoice.B: T-cell ALL is usually positive for CD2, CD3, CD4, CD7, and CD8 rather than the B-cell markers shown.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Acute myeloid leukemia expresses myeloid markers and does not express this B-lymphoid, TdT-positive profile.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia is a mature B-cell disorder and is TdT negative.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Multiple myeloma is a plasma cell neoplasm and does not express a TdT-positive lymphoblast profile.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman with multiple sclerosis is hospitalized for a flare and treated with high-dose corticosteroids. She now feels well, and her temperature has been normal throughout admission with an unremarkable physical examination. Morning laboratory studies show a white blood cell count of 16,000/mm3 with 92% mature segmented neutrophils. Which of the following best explains her elevated neutrophil count?

explanation.summary: Corticosteroids cause a benign mature neutrophilia by driving marginated neutrophils into the circulating pool (demargination).
explanation.whyCorrect: In the absence of signs or symptoms of infection, an elevated count of mature segmented neutrophils in a patient on high-dose steroids reflects benign leukocytosis. Steroids and stress cause marginated neutrophils pressed against the vessel wall to enter the bloodstream, a process called demargination; because roughly half of the body's neutrophils are normally marginated, this can significantly raise the count without any increase in marrow production. It is not a serious concern and requires no further testing.

whyWrongByChoice.B: There are no signs or symptoms of infection by history or physical examination, and the cells are all mature without a left shift.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chronic myeloid leukemia typically produces a markedly high WBC count and is often accompanied by basophilia, not a modest steroid-associated rise in mature neutrophils.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A leukoerythroblastic reaction shows immature neutrophils and nucleated red blood cells, which are not present here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acute lymphoblastic leukemia produces circulating lymphoblasts with dangerously low normal WBCs, not a predominance of mature neutrophils.

questions.stem: A hematologist defines a laboratory finding as an elevated white blood cell count in the peripheral blood, greater than 11,000 cells/mm3, that is caused by something other than a neoplasm. Which of the following terms best describes this finding?

explanation.summary: Benign leukocytosis is an elevated peripheral WBC count (>11,000/mm3) caused by something other than neoplasms.
explanation.whyCorrect: Leukocytosis means the WBC count is above the normal range; benign leukocytosis specifically denotes an elevation greater than 11,000 cells/mm3 that is not due to a hematopoietic neoplasm. The most common cause is infection, and other causes include drugs, inflammatory diseases, and stress.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Neoplastic leukocytosis is caused by bone marrow neoplasms and is often associated with very high WBC counts of 100,000/mm3 or more.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Leukopenia is a decreased WBC count, the opposite of leukocytosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A leukoerythroblastic reaction is one specific pattern of immature neutrophilia, not the general term for a non-neoplastic elevated count.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Neutropenia refers to a depletion of neutrophils, not an elevated total WBC count.

questions.stem: A 46-year-old woman presents with fever, sweating, and fatigue. Her complete blood count shows a WBC count of 18,000/mm3 with 60% neutrophils. The blood smear reveals occasional bands, metamyelocytes, and promyelocytes, along with many mature segmented neutrophils containing dark cytoplasmic granules. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her findings?

explanation.summary: Neutrophilia with a left shift and toxic granulation is characteristic of a leukemoid reaction to bacterial infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient has neutrophilia with a left shift (immature neutrophil forms such as bands, metamyelocytes, and promyelocytes), characteristic of a leukemoid reaction due to infection or inflammation. The dark granules represent toxic granulation, which is most consistent with a bacterial infection and accounts for her fever.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Acute leukemia would show numerous circulating blast cells rather than a maturing left shift with toxic granulation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chronic myeloid leukemia typically shows a markedly high WBC count, usually more than 100,000/mm3.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Parasitic infection is typically associated with eosinophilia, not neutrophilia with toxic changes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reactive lymphocytosis involves a proliferation of reactive lymphocytes, not neutrophils with a left shift.

questions.stem: A patient admitted to the hospital has a blood smear showing a left shift. Which of the following cell types is most likely to be present in increased numbers?

explanation.summary: A left shift refers to the presence of immature neutrophils (bands, metamyelocytes, promyelocytes) in the peripheral blood.
explanation.whyCorrect: Normally only mature segmented neutrophils are seen in the blood. A left shift means neutrophil precursors are present in the circulation, most commonly as a response to infection.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Basophils are elevated in allergies, myeloproliferative disorders, hypothyroidism, and certain infections, not in a left shift.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Eosinophils are elevated in allergies, drug reactions, asthma, and parasitic worm infections.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Monocytes are occasionally elevated in conditions such as chronic infection or inflammation but do not define a left shift.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reactive lymphocytes are seen primarily in viral infections, not in a neutrophil left shift.

questions.stem: An asymptomatic patient has a routine complete blood count showing a total leukocyte count of 10,000 cells/mm3. The differential shows 50% neutrophils, 45% lymphocytes, 3% monocytes, 1% eosinophils, and 1% basophils. The blood smear reveals normal-appearing cells. Which of the following disorders is most consistent with these findings?

explanation.summary: A normal total WBC count with an elevated absolute lymphocyte count (>4000/mm3) indicates lymphocytosis, and normal-appearing cells make it a non-reactive lymphocytosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The total leukocyte count is normal at 10,000/mm3, but 45% lymphocytes yields an absolute lymphocyte count of 4500, above the normal upper limit of about 4000. This is lymphocytosis. Because the cells appear normal under the microscope, it is not a reactive lymphocytosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The absolute neutrophil count (5000) is normal (below 7700), so immature neutrophilia is not present.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The absolute neutrophil count is normal, so mature neutrophilia is not present either.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The monocyte count is normal, making monocytosis incorrect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reactive lymphocytosis would show abnormal reactive lymphocyte forms, whereas the cells here appear normal.

questions.stem: A neutrophil on a blood smear from a septic patient contains numerous small, clear, membrane-bound bubbles in its cytoplasm. This toxic change occurs by which of the following mechanisms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Degranulation of lysosomes within the neutrophil

choices.label: B
choices.text: Accumulation of ribosomes and rough endoplasmic reticulum

choices.label: D
choices.text: Release of nucleated red blood cells from the marrow

choices.label: E
choices.text: Demargination of neutrophils from the vessel wall

explanation.summary: Cytoplasmic vacuolization results from degranulation of lysosomes within neutrophils and is typically seen in sepsis.
explanation.whyCorrect: A vacuole is a membrane-bound vesicle in the cytoplasm. Cytoplasmic vacuolization occurs due to degranulation of lysosomes within the neutrophils, appearing as small clear bubbles. The process usually occurs in cases of sepsis, when there is widespread destruction of pathogens inside the neutrophils.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Ribosomes and rough endoplasmic reticulum remnants describe Dohle bodies, not vacuolization.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increased primary granules containing peroxidases and hydrolases describe toxic granulation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Release of nucleated red blood cells is characteristic of a leukoerythroblastic reaction, not vacuolization.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Demargination raises the mature neutrophil count but does not create cytoplasmic vacuoles.

questions.stem: A blood smear from a patient with a bacterial infection shows neutrophils containing small blue-gray oval inclusions located at the periphery of the cytoplasm. These inclusions are composed of which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ribosomes and remnants of rough endoplasmic reticulum

choices.label: B
choices.text: Peroxidases and hydrolases within primary granules

choices.label: D
choices.text: Crystalline aggregates of fused azurophilic granules

choices.label: E
choices.text: Fragments of nucleated red blood cells

explanation.summary: Dohle bodies are blue-gray peripheral cytoplasmic inclusions made of ribosomes and remnants of rough endoplasmic reticulum.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dohle bodies are small circular or oval blue-gray cytoplasmic inclusions usually located at the periphery of the neutrophil. They consist of ribosomes and remnants of rough endoplasmic reticulum and are often a manifestation of the increased protein production in neutrophils responding to a bacterial infection.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Peroxidases and hydrolases in primary granules describe toxic granulation, not Dohle bodies.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Membrane-bound vesicles from lysosomal degranulation describe cytoplasmic vacuolization.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Crystalline granule aggregates (Auer rods) are seen in myeloid blasts, not as Dohle bodies.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nucleated red blood cell fragments are not the composition of Dohle bodies.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old college student presents with fever, sore throat, and cervical lymphadenopathy. A blood smear shows an increased number of large lymphocytes with abundant, agranular cytoplasm and a fried-egg appearance that molds around adjacent red blood cells. These cells are most characteristic of infection with which of the following?

explanation.summary: Downey type II reactive lymphocytes are most commonly associated with Epstein-Barr virus (infectious mononucleosis).
explanation.whyCorrect: Downey cells are a specific type of reactive lymphocyte. Downey type II cells are the most common type and are often associated with Epstein-Barr virus. They are larger cells, often with a fried-egg appearance, and have abundant agranular cytoplasm that abuts or molds around adjacent erythrocytes.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Bordetella pertussis causes lymphocytosis with normal-appearing lymphocytes, not reactive Downey cells.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Coxsackievirus causes infectious lymphocytosis with normal-appearing lymphocytes, typically in children.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mycobacterium tuberculosis can cause lymphocytosis but is not the classic cause of Downey type II cells.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Staphylococcus aureus causes a neutrophilic response with toxic changes, not reactive lymphocytosis.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with a history of asthma and seasonal allergies is evaluated for an elevated white blood cell count, and his differential shows an increased eosinophil count. Besides allergies, asthma, and drug reactions, which of the following is most classically associated with eosinophilia?

explanation.summary: Eosinophilia (>500/mm3) is classically associated with allergies, drug reactions, asthma, and helminthic (parasitic worm) infections.
explanation.whyCorrect: An elevated eosinophil count is fairly common and is associated with many conditions, including allergies, drug reactions, asthma, and helminthic (parasitic worm) infections. Of the options, parasitic worm infection is the classic infectious cause of eosinophilia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Bacterial infection classically causes neutrophilia, not eosinophilia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Viral infection classically raises the lymphocyte count.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hypothyroidism is a classic cause of basophilia, not eosinophilia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acute stress causes neutrophilia via demargination or a transient lymphocytosis, not eosinophilia.

questions.stem: A previously healthy man is found to have persistent basophilia on repeated complete blood counts, with no evidence of allergy, hypothyroidism, or recent infection. This finding is classically associated with which of the following neoplastic disorders?

explanation.summary: Basophilia is the least common benign leukocytosis but is classically seen in myeloproliferative disorders, particularly chronic myeloid leukemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Basophilia (>300/mm3) is the least common type of benign leukocytosis, with benign causes including allergies, hypothyroidism, and certain infections. There are also neoplastic diseases (myeloproliferative disorders) that classically show basophilia, particularly chronic myeloid leukemia and, to a lesser extent, polycythemia vera.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Acute lymphoblastic leukemia produces circulating lymphoblasts, not a characteristic basophilia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Follicular lymphoma is a mature B-cell lymphoma and is not associated with basophilia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Multiple myeloma is a plasma cell neoplasm and does not classically cause basophilia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Infectious mononucleosis causes a reactive lymphocytosis, not basophilia.

questions.stem: A patient with severe anemia has a blood smear showing both immature neutrophils and nucleated (immature) red blood cells in the peripheral blood. Which of the following best describes this pattern of benign immature neutrophilia?

explanation.summary: A leukoerythroblastic reaction is defined by immature neutrophils together with nucleated red blood cells in the peripheral blood.
explanation.whyCorrect: In a leukoerythroblastic reaction (sometimes called a myelophthisic process), there are two kinds of immature cells in the blood: immature neutrophils and immature (nucleated) red blood cells. This can represent a normal bone marrow response, most often in severe anemia when the marrow is operating at high speed, but it can also occur when a neoplasm or fibrosis fills the marrow so cells leave early.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A leukemoid reaction includes immature neutrophils and their precursors but not nucleated red blood cells.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Benign mature neutrophilia shows only normal mature segmented neutrophils, with no immature forms.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Demargination shifts existing mature neutrophils into the circulation and does not release nucleated red cells.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reactive lymphocytosis involves reactive lymphocytes, not immature neutrophils and nucleated red cells.

questions.stem: A clinician notes that in most conditions, relying only on differential percentages can be misleading, and that a particular value is generally most important in diagnosing conditions. Which of the following is generally most important?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The absolute counts of the individual WBC types

choices.label: B
choices.text: The percentages of the individual WBC types

choices.label: D
choices.text: The ratio of neutrophils to lymphocytes

choices.label: E
choices.text: The morphology of red blood cells

explanation.summary: Absolute counts, not percentages, are generally most important in diagnosing most conditions.
explanation.whyCorrect: Although differential percentages are important and easy to use, they can sometimes lead you astray if you do not pay attention to the absolute values. In general, the absolute counts, not the percentages, are most important in diagnosing most conditions. The absolute count is obtained by multiplying the total WBC count by the percentage of each type.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Percentages alone can be misleading because a normal percentage can correspond to an abnormal absolute number and vice versa.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The total WBC count alone does not reveal which specific cell line is increased or decreased.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio is not the value the brick emphasizes for diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Red blood cell morphology is not the WBC value used to interpret the differential.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman is evaluated for a neck mass. On examination, several enlarged cervical lymph nodes are palpated. Which of the following physical findings would most strongly suggest a benign, rather than neoplastic, cause of her lymphadenopathy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nodes that are firm and rubbery-hard

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nodes that are fixed to the underlying tissue

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nodes that are soft, freely movable, and tender

choices.label: E
choices.text: Nodes that are immobile on palpation

explanation.summary: Benign lymphadenopathy typically produces nodes that are soft, smooth, freely movable, and often tender.
explanation.whyCorrect: In benign lymphadenopathy, nodes are generally soft, smooth, freely moveable, and often tender because the enlargement results from reactive hyperplasia rather than infiltrative growth. Neoplastic nodes, by contrast, tend to be firm, immobile, fixed to the underlying tissue, and painless.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Firm, hard nodes are characteristic of neoplastic rather than benign lymphadenopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fixation to underlying tissue is a feature that favors a neoplastic process, not a benign one.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Painless nodes are typical of malignancy; benign reactive nodes are often tender.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Immobility suggests infiltration by tumor and points away from a benign cause.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man is found to have an enlarged, firm lymph node just above the left clavicle. It is nontender and fixed. Enlargement of this particular node should most prompt a search for a malignancy in which of the following locations?

explanation.summary: The left supraclavicular (Virchow) node drains the abdomen and its enlargement suggests abdominal, especially gastric, cancer.
explanation.whyCorrect: The left supraclavicular node, also called the Virchow node, receives abdominal lymphatics that drain into the thoracic duct and then into the left supraclavicular node. An enlarged node here should prompt a search for an abdominal cancer, particularly gastric cancer.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Chest malignancy such as lung cancer is suggested by an enlarged right supraclavicular node, not the left.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Oral and pharyngeal disease points to submandibular or submental nodes, not the supraclavicular region.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Breast cancer classically involves axillary nodes rather than the left supraclavicular node.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Upper extremity infection drains to axillary nodes, not to the supraclavicular area.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman has a dry cough and mild fatigue. A chest radiograph shows symmetric bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following conditions is most consistent with this pattern of adenopathy?

explanation.summary: Hilar lymphadenopathy classically points to lung infection, lung cancer, or the granulomatous condition sarcoidosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hilar lymphadenopathy most often indicates a lung infection, lung cancer, or sarcoidosis, and sarcoidosis classically presents with bilateral hilar adenopathy. This granulomatous disease is a well-recognized benign cause of adenopathy in this location.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A dental abscess causes submandibular or submental adenopathy, not hilar enlargement.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Gastric carcinoma would be suggested by a left supraclavicular (Virchow) node rather than bilateral hilar nodes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Toxoplasmosis typically causes generalized or head-and-neck adenopathy, not an isolated bilateral hilar pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Typhoid fever produces mesenteric adenopathy rather than hilar lymphadenopathy.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman develops diffuse lymph node enlargement two weeks after starting a new medication for a seizure disorder. She also has a low-grade fever and joint aches resembling serum sickness. Which type of hypersensitivity reaction most likely underlies this drug-induced generalized adenopathy?

explanation.summary: Many drugs cause generalized adenopathy through a serum-sickness-like, type 3 hypersensitivity reaction.
explanation.whyCorrect: The article notes that drugs most commonly cause generalized adenopathy via a serum-sickness-like hypersensitivity reaction, which is a type 3 (immune complex) hypersensitivity. Antiseizure medications such as carbamazepine, lamotrigine, and primidone are among the drugs implicated.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Type 1 hypersensitivity mediates anaphylaxis and allergic reactions, not serum-sickness-like generalized adenopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Type 2 hypersensitivity involves antibody-mediated cell destruction, which is not the mechanism described here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Type 4 hypersensitivity is delayed and T-cell mediated, whereas the serum-sickness-like drug reaction is immune complex driven.
whyWrongByChoice.E: IgA-mediated mucosal reactions are not the mechanism of drug-induced generalized lymphadenopathy.

questions.stem: A lymph node biopsy from a patient with a chronic infection shows enlarged follicles containing prominent germinal centers with tingible body macrophages. This pattern of benign follicular hyperplasia is driven predominantly by proliferation of which of the following cell types?

explanation.summary: Benign follicular hyperplasia reflects B-cell proliferation within lymph node follicles.
explanation.whyCorrect: Benign follicular hyperplasia is caused by B-cell proliferation in the lymph node follicles in response to an immune stimulus. The stimulus converts small primary follicles into enlarged secondary follicles that develop germinal centers containing proliferating lymphocytes and tingible body macrophages.

whyWrongByChoice.B: T-cell proliferation drives interfollicular/paracortical hyperplasia, not follicular hyperplasia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Neutrophils are agents of acute suppurative inflammation and are not the basis of follicular hyperplasia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Plasma cells may be present but are not the proliferating population that defines follicular hyperplasia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Fibroblasts contribute to fibrosis, not to the reactive follicular hyperplasia of an immune response.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man with a recent viral illness has a lymph node biopsy showing diffuse expansion of the paracortex and the areas between the follicles. A predominant proliferation of which of the following cell types best explains this reactive pattern?

explanation.summary: Benign interfollicular/paracortical hyperplasia represents a predominant T-cell response.
explanation.whyCorrect: Benign interfollicular/paracortical hyperplasia represents a predominant T-cell (not B-cell) response to an immune stimulus, and the lymph node shows expansion of the paracortex and the areas between follicles. Causes of this paracortical pattern include viral infection, vaccination, reaction to a nearby malignancy, and autoimmune disease.

whyWrongByChoice.B: B-cell proliferation produces follicular hyperplasia with germinal centers, not paracortical expansion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mast cells mediate allergic responses and do not drive paracortical hyperplasia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Endothelial cells line vessels and are not the proliferating population in this reactive pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Osteoclasts are bone-resorbing cells and have no role in lymph node paracortical hyperplasia.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man develops an infected laceration on his right hand after a gardening injury. Over the next few days he notices tender swelling in the ipsilateral armpit. Enlargement of which group of lymph nodes best explains this finding?

explanation.summary: The axillary nodes drain the upper extremity and enlarge with an arm or hand infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Axillary nodes drain the upper extremities, so an infection of the hand or arm produces regional axillary adenopathy. A localized infection often presents with related regional lymphadenopathy in the nodes that drain the affected site.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hilar nodes drain the lungs and are unrelated to a hand infection.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Submental nodes drain the floor of the mouth and chin, not the upper extremity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mesenteric nodes drain intra-abdominal structures and are seen with conditions such as typhoid fever.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The left supraclavicular node drains the abdomen and would not enlarge from a hand infection.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man is evaluated for enlarged cervical lymph nodes that appeared shortly after he began atenolol for newly diagnosed hypertension. He has no fever, no weight loss, and no other findings; examination and laboratory studies show no evidence of infection or malignancy. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Start anti-inflammatory therapy for an autoimmune disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform excisional biopsy of a cervical node now

explanation.summary: Management of benign lymphadenopathy is tailored to the cause; drug-induced adenopathy resolves after stopping the offending drug.
explanation.whyCorrect: Management is tailored to the underlying cause, and treatment is based on eliminating infections, stopping offending drugs, and addressing inflammatory conditions. When the clinical picture points to a drug such as atenolol as the cause, stopping the offending drug will likely lead to resolution of the lymphadenopathy.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Antibiotics treat adenopathy secondary to infection, but no infection is identified in this case.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Anti-inflammatory therapy addresses an underlying inflammatory disorder, which is not present here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Excisional biopsy is reserved for adenopathy that does not resolve with initial measures or that raises concern for malignancy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Systemic corticosteroids are not the initial step; removing the offending drug is the appropriate action.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman presents with a single, 1.5 cm, movable, rubbery lymph node in her right axilla. She feels no breast lump, and the rest of the examination is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?

explanation.summary: A single enlarged axillary node in a woman warrants breast evaluation to exclude breast cancer.
explanation.whyCorrect: A single enlarged axillary node suggests pathology in the arm or chest wall, and in women breast cancer is an obvious consideration because the axillary nodes drain the breast. A thorough breast exam and mammography are the appropriate next steps so that malignancy can be excluded, with biopsy reserved for later if these are unrevealing.

whyWrongByChoice.B: HIV testing is appropriate for unexplained generalized adenopathy, not for a single localized axillary node.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Lymph node biopsy would only be done after routine testing such as mammography, not as the immediate first step.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Stopping medications is used for drug-induced adenopathy, which typically causes diffuse rather than single-node enlargement.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reassurance without workup is inappropriate because a solitary axillary node in a woman requires exclusion of breast cancer.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man has enlarged cervical lymph nodes together with a 6 kg unintentional weight loss and drenching night sweats over the past two months. On examination, a firm node is fixed to underlying tissue. Which of the following is the most appropriate step on initial workup?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prescribe a course of oral antibiotics

explanation.summary: Signs or symptoms of cancer, such as weight loss and a fixed node, warrant lymph node biopsy on initial workup.
explanation.whyCorrect: A lymph node biopsy is indicated on initial workup if the patient has underlying signs or symptoms of cancer, such as weight loss, fever, or a palpable tumor outside the nodes. The goal is to exclude neoplastic causes of the lymphadenopathy, which this patient's weight loss and fixed, firm node raise concern for.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A four-week period of observation is appropriate for unexplained local adenopathy without worrisome features, not for adenopathy with cancer warning signs.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Empiric antibiotics would only be reasonable if infection were suspected, not when malignancy is the concern.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Stopping medications addresses drug-induced adenopathy and does not fit a patient with weight loss and a fixed node.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A heterophile test evaluates for mononucleosis and would not address the concern for malignancy raised by his systemic symptoms.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man has enlargement of noncontiguous cervical, axillary, and inguinal lymph nodes without an obvious localizing source. History and physical examination do not reveal the cause. Which of the following sets of tests is the most appropriate initial evaluation for this generalized adenopathy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Complete blood count, HIV test, and chest x-ray

choices.label: D
choices.text: Serum ferritin and total iron-binding capacity

explanation.summary: Generalized adenopathy is initially evaluated with tests such as a CBC, HIV test, and chest x-ray.
explanation.whyCorrect: For generalized adenopathy, testing may include a CBC, HIV test, and chest x-ray, with testing for tuberculosis and EBV also conducted. If all of these are negative, a biopsy may then be done on an easily reached node in the neck or supraclavicular area.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Mammography targets a single axillary node in a woman with possible breast cancer, not diffuse generalized adenopathy in a young man.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bone marrow biopsy is not the routine first step; initial testing screens for infection and systemic disease first.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Iron studies evaluate anemia and are unrelated to the workup of generalized lymphadenopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Upper endoscopy pursues gastric disease suggested by a Virchow node, not undifferentiated generalized adenopathy.

questions.stem: A histopathologist examines a reactive lymph node and points out large phagocytic cells within the germinal center that contain engulfed cellular debris, described as tingible body macrophages. In which region of the lymph node are these cells and their surrounding germinal center normally located?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Within a secondary follicle of the follicular region

choices.label: B
choices.text: Within the paracortex among the T cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Within the hilum near the efferent vessel

explanation.summary: Germinal centers with tingible body macrophages reside in secondary follicles of the follicular (B-cell) region.
explanation.whyCorrect: The follicular region contains B cells arranged in follicles, and secondary follicles contain a germinal center that has been antigenically stimulated. The germinal center holds proliferating lymphocytes along with tingible body macrophages that are busy cleaning up cellular debris.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The paracortex is the T-cell zone that surrounds the follicles and does not house germinal centers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The subcapsular sinus is a lymphatic channel beneath the capsule, not the site of germinal centers.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The medullary cords are deep cords rich in plasma cells, not the location of germinal-center macrophages.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The hilum is where vessels enter and leave the node and does not contain germinal centers.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old wildlife biologist presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of sudden high fever, chills, and a painful swelling in his right groin. He works outdoors in rural New Mexico and reports handling a dead prairie dog without gloves several days ago. On examination he appears acutely ill and has a tender, markedly enlarged inguinal lymph node. Which of the following is the most likely etiologic agent?

explanation.summary: Sudden fever with a tender bubo after rodent exposure in the western US is classic bubonic plague.
explanation.whyCorrect: Y. pestis is endemic in the western United States, where rodents, prairie dogs, and squirrels are natural reservoirs and the flea is the main vector. Bubonic plague, the most common form, follows a flea bite or contact with an infected animal or carcass and produces a massively enlarged, tender lymph node (bubo) with sudden fever and chills.

whyWrongByChoice.A: F. tularensis typically produces an ulcer at the inoculation site and is found predominantly in the Ozarks after rabbit or tick exposure, not prairie dog handling in the western US.
whyWrongByChoice.C: B. henselae causes milder lymphadenopathy after a cat scratch or bite, not an acute febrile bubo after rodent contact.
whyWrongByChoice.D: C. trachomatis L1-L3 causes lymphogranuloma venereum, a sexually transmitted infection with buboes, not a zoonotic exposure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Human herpesvirus-8 causes Kaposi sarcoma, not an acute febrile lymphadenopathy after rodent contact.

questions.topicName: Y. pestis, F. tularensis, and B. henselae

questions.stem: A patient with suspected plague has an aspirate taken from an enlarged inguinal lymph node. The sample is sent for staining. Which of the following staining patterns would most strongly support a diagnosis of Yersinia pestis?

explanation.summary: Y. pestis is a gram-negative bacillus that shows a distinctive bipolar 'safety-pin' staining pattern.
explanation.whyCorrect: Y. pestis is a non-motile, gram-negative facultative anaerobe. With select stains, the dye concentrates at the ends of the bacillus, giving a bipolar appearance that resembles a safety pin. Patient samples such as bubo aspirates, blood, or sputum are examined for this pattern.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Gram-positive cocci in chains suggest Streptococcus species, not Y. pestis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Gram-positive cocci in clusters suggest Staphylococcus species, not a gram-negative plague bacillus.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Gram-negative diplococci suggest organisms such as Neisseria, not the bipolar bacillus of plague.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acid-fast bacilli suggest mycobacteria; Y. pestis is not acid-fast.

questions.topicName: Y. pestis, F. tularensis, and B. henselae

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man with HIV and a CD4 count of 40 cells/uL presents with fever and multiple red-purple, dome-shaped skin nodules on his trunk and arms that have gradually increased over the past month. The lesions are tender and bleed easily. Biopsy stain reveals small gram-negative bacilli, and histology shows neutrophilic infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely etiologic agent?
