explanation.summary: Vascular skin nodules in a severely immunocompromised host caused by a gram-negative bacillus indicate bacillary angiomatosis from B. henselae.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bacillary angiomatosis is seen only in severely immunocompromised patients, such as HIV patients with T-cell counts below 100 cells/uL. It presents with multiple disseminated bright red or purple vascular skin nodules. B. henselae lesions are tender, bleed easily, and show neutrophilic infiltrates, distinguishing them from Kaposi sarcoma.

whyWrongByChoice.B: HHV-8 causes Kaposi sarcoma, which resembles bacillary angiomatosis but is caused by a virus (not a gram-negative bacillus); its lesions are usually painless, do not bleed, and show lymphocytic infiltrates.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Y. pestis causes plague with buboes and pneumonia, not chronic vascular skin nodules.
whyWrongByChoice.D: F. tularensis causes ulceroglandular, pneumonic, or typhoidal disease, not disseminated vascular nodules.
whyWrongByChoice.E: C. trachomatis L1-L3 causes lymphogranuloma venereum with genital ulcers and buboes, not vascular skin nodules.

questions.topicName: Y. pestis, F. tularensis, and B. henselae

questions.stem: A 45-year-old hunter in Arkansas presents with fever, chills, and a painful swollen lymph node in his right axilla. A few days earlier he skinned a wild rabbit and later noticed a small ulcer with an erythematous base on the dorsum of his right hand. Which of the following is the most likely etiologic agent?

explanation.summary: A skin ulcer at the inoculation site plus regional lymphadenopathy after rabbit exposure in the Ozarks is classic ulceroglandular tularemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: F. tularensis is transmitted by direct contact with rabbits or by tick and deer fly bites, and skinning a rabbit can aerosolize or inoculate the bacteria. Ulceroglandular disease is the most common form (75-85%) and uniquely causes ulceration at the inoculation site with enlarged regional lymph nodes. Most US cases occur in the Ozarks (Arkansas and Missouri).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Y. pestis causes a bubo but classically does not ulcerate at the inoculation site and is found in the western US after rodent exposure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: B. henselae follows a cat scratch or bite, not rabbit skinning, and causes milder lymphadenopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: S. aureus can cause skin infection and lymphadenopathy but does not fit the rabbit exposure and classic ulceroglandular pattern in an endemic area.
whyWrongByChoice.E: C. trachomatis L1-L3 is sexually transmitted and causes genital ulcers, not a hand ulcer from rabbit contact.

questions.topicName: Y. pestis, F. tularensis, and B. henselae

questions.stem: The F1 antigen capsule is an important virulence factor of Yersinia pestis. Which of the following best describes a distinguishing property of this capsule?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is composed predominantly of protein rather than polysaccharide

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is produced at 25 degrees C within the flea vector

choices.label: C
choices.text: It enhances phagocytosis of the bacterium by neutrophils

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is encoded on the bacterial chromosome rather than a plasmid

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is composed of lipopolysaccharide endotoxin

explanation.summary: Unlike most encapsulated bacteria, the Y. pestis capsule is protein-based, built predominantly from the F1 antigen.
explanation.whyCorrect: What distinguishes the Y. pestis capsule from most other bacterial capsules is that it is protein-based, constructed predominantly from the F1 antigen (a protein), rather than the polysaccharide typical of most encapsulated bacteria.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The capsule is only produced at 37 degrees C, not at the flea's 25 degrees C body temperature, allowing the bacterium to conserve energy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The capsule is anti-phagocytic and conceals surface antigens, providing early immune evasion, not enhanced phagocytosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The capsule is encoded on a plasmid, not the chromosome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: LPS is a separate gram-negative endotoxin; the capsule is made from the F1 antigen protein.

questions.topicName: Y. pestis, F. tularensis, and B. henselae

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man is diagnosed with septicemic plague and is critically ill with hypotension and evidence of disseminated intravascular coagulation. Which of the following is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic for this severe infection?

explanation.summary: Severe or systemic plague is treated first-line with an intravenous aminoglycoside such as gentamicin.
explanation.whyCorrect: For severe disease presentations such as septicemic or pneumonic plague, the first-line therapy is intravenous aminoglycosides, typically gentamicin, which provide reliable bactericidal activity in life-threatening infections.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Azithromycin is a first-line agent for cat scratch disease, not for severe plague.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Penicillin V is a beta-lactam that does not provide coverage against plague.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Doxycycline is effective for plague but is reserved for mild, non-severe cases or when other options are contraindicated.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ciprofloxacin (a fluoroquinolone) is an option for mild, stable cases, not the preferred agent for severe disease.

questions.topicName: Y. pestis, F. tularensis, and B. henselae

questions.stem: Yersinia pestis is a facultative intracellular pathogen that can survive attacks by phagocytes in the lymph nodes. Which of the following mechanisms best explains how Y. pestis directly delivers immunosuppressive effector proteins into host phagocytes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A type III secretion system that injects Yops into host cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Secretion of a polysaccharide capsule that lyses phagocytes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Production of an exotoxin that ADP-ribosylates elongation factor 2

choices.label: D
choices.text: Release of coagulase that forms a protective fibrin clot

choices.label: E
choices.text: Formation of a biofilm that physically excludes neutrophils

explanation.summary: Y. pestis uses a syringe-like type III secretion system to inject Yersinia outer proteins (Yops) into phagocytes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Yops are delivered to host cells via type III secretion systems (T3SS), transmembrane protein complexes that function like a syringe. As phagocytes approach, Y. pestis uses the T3SS to inject Yops into those cells, effectively neutralizing them, which is why the bacterium can fight the immune system from within the lymph nodes.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The capsule is anti-phagocytic and conceals antigens, but it does not lyse phagocytes or deliver effector proteins.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The article does not attribute plague virulence to an EF-2-inactivating exotoxin; effectors are delivered by the T3SS.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Coagulase and fibrin clot formation are not the described mechanism for Yop delivery.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Biofilm exclusion of neutrophils is not the described mechanism; Yops are actively injected via the T3SS.

questions.topicName: Y. pestis, F. tularensis, and B. henselae

questions.stem: A laboratory attempts to culture Francisella tularensis from a patient with suspected tularemia. Which of the following is a specific requirement for successful growth of this organism in culture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Media supplemented with cysteine, often charcoal agar

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anaerobic incubation with added vitamin K and hemin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chocolate agar supplemented with factors V and X

choices.label: E
choices.text: Charcoal-yeast extract agar with iron and cysteine at 42 degrees C for Legionella

explanation.summary: F. tularensis culture requires cysteine, often on charcoal agar, and must be handled carefully because of its low infectious dose.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because infection can occur with a very small inoculum, culture of F. tularensis is dangerous and must be done with cysteine, often in charcoal agar; this is a requirement for the organism to grow. F. tularensis is a BSL3 pathogen, so labs must be alerted when it is suspected.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Anaerobic incubation with vitamin K and hemin is used for organisms such as Bacteroides, not F. tularensis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chocolate agar with factors V and X supports Haemophilus influenzae, not F. tularensis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tellurite agar is used for Corynebacterium diphtheriae, not tularemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The brick specifies cysteine and charcoal agar for F. tularensis and does not describe a Legionella-specific protocol as the requirement.

questions.topicName: Y. pestis, F. tularensis, and B. henselae

questions.stem: A patient develops severe pneumonia with a productive cough and bloody sputum after close contact with another person who had plague pneumonia. Which of the following best explains why pneumonic plague, unlike pneumonic tularemia, is a major public health concern for person-to-person spread?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Once Y. pestis actively replicates in the lungs, it spreads person-to-person via respiratory droplets

choices.label: B
choices.text: Y. pestis is transmitted person-to-person only through contaminated blood products

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pneumonic plague is transmitted exclusively by flea bites indoors

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pneumonic tularemia spreads more efficiently between people than pneumonic plague

choices.label: E
choices.text: Y. pestis requires an arthropod vector to move between human hosts

explanation.summary: Pneumonic plague can spread directly between people by respiratory droplets, making prompt treatment critical.
explanation.whyCorrect: Once Y. pestis is actively replicating in the lungs, it can spread person-to-person via respiratory droplets. Pneumonic plague causes severe pneumonia with necrotization of lung tissue and a productive cough with bloody sputum (hemoptysis), and prompt treatment is critical to prevent spread.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Transmission is via respiratory droplets from active lung infection, not exclusively contaminated blood products.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pneumonic plague is acquired by aerosolized droplets or blood spread to the lungs, not exclusively indoor flea bites.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Unlike pneumonic plague, there is no significant person-to-person transmission of tularemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Respiratory droplet spread does not require an arthropod vector between human hosts.

questions.topicName: Y. pestis, F. tularensis, and B. henselae

questions.stem: A 9-year-old girl presents with a low-grade fever and a tender, enlarged lymph node in her right axilla. Two weeks earlier she developed a small papule on her right forearm after playing with a new kitten. She is otherwise healthy. Which of the following is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Three months of doxycycline plus erythromycin

explanation.summary: Cat scratch disease in an immunocompetent patient is treated first-line with the macrolide azithromycin.
explanation.whyCorrect: This is cat scratch disease from B. henselae, presenting with a low-grade fever, an inoculation papule (3-10 days), and regional lymphadenopathy (1-3 weeks) in an axillary node after a kitten scratch. In otherwise healthy individuals, first-line therapy is a short course of the macrolide azithromycin, which helps accelerate resolution of the lymphadenitis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Intravenous gentamicin is reserved for severe plague and tularemia, not uncomplicated cat scratch disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Lymph node aspiration is not recommended for cases of cat scratch disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Prolonged doxycycline or erythromycin is used for bacillary angiomatosis in immunocompromised patients, not cat scratch disease in a healthy child.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Penicillin V does not provide appropriate coverage for B. henselae.

questions.topicName: Y. pestis, F. tularensis, and B. henselae

questions.stem: Public health officials list Francisella tularensis as a potential agent of bioterrorism. Which of the following properties of F. tularensis most contributes to this concern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An extremely low infectious dose, as few as 10-50 bacteria

choices.label: C
choices.text: Resistance to all aminoglycosides and fluoroquinolones

choices.label: D
choices.text: An infectious dose of 1000-2000 organisms, similar to influenza A

explanation.summary: F. tularensis can infect with a very small inoculum and can be aerosolized, making it a bioterrorism concern.
explanation.whyCorrect: A very small number of F. tularensis bacteria are needed to cause infection, as low as 10-50 bacteria. It can be aerosolized (for example, by skinning a rabbit) and could be aerosolized for weaponization, which is why it is considered an agent of bioterrorism.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Unlike pneumonic plague, there is no significant person-to-person transmission of tularemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: F. tularensis is treatable with aminoglycosides and fluoroquinolones; it is not pan-resistant.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An infectious dose of 1000-2000 organisms describes influenza A virus (ID50), not F. tularensis, whose dose is far lower.
whyWrongByChoice.E: F. tularensis can readily be aerosolized, which is precisely part of the weaponization concern.

questions.topicName: Y. pestis, F. tularensis, and B. henselae

questions.stem: A pathologist is asked to distinguish bacillary angiomatosis from Kaposi sarcoma in an immunocompromised patient with red-purple skin lesions. Which of the following findings would most strongly favor bacillary angiomatosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tender lesions that bleed easily with neutrophilic infiltrates

choices.label: B
choices.text: Painless lesions that do not bleed with lymphocytic infiltrates

choices.label: D
choices.text: Absence of any identifiable bacteria on tissue stains

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lesions that occur only in immunocompetent hosts

explanation.summary: Bacillary angiomatosis lesions are tender, bleed easily, and show neutrophilic infiltrates, unlike Kaposi sarcoma.
explanation.whyCorrect: The skin nodules of bacillary angiomatosis can resemble Kaposi sarcoma, but they are distinguished by their lesions: BA lesions are tender, bleed easily, and have neutrophilic infiltrates. BA is caused by the gram-negative bacillus B. henselae.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Painless lesions that do not bleed and show lymphocytic infiltrates describe Kaposi sarcoma, not bacillary angiomatosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: HHV-8 positivity indicates Kaposi sarcoma, which is caused by that virus rather than B. henselae.
whyWrongByChoice.D: BA is caused by a gram-negative bacillus that can be demonstrated in tissue; absence of bacteria would argue against it.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Both bacillary angiomatosis and Kaposi sarcoma occur in severely immunocompromised patients, so this does not distinguish them.

questions.topicName: Y. pestis, F. tularensis, and B. henselae

questions.stem: A public health officer is counseling residents of a flood-affected tropical region on how to reduce their risk of acquiring Leptospira infection. Which of the following measures is most effective in preventing human infection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Avoiding wading through or swimming in contaminated freshwater

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wearing gloves when handling raw poultry

explanation.summary: Leptospira spreads through water, soil, or food contaminated with the urine of infected animals, so avoiding contaminated freshwater is the key preventive measure.
explanation.whyCorrect: Transmission occurs when people are exposed directly or indirectly to the urine or body fluids of infected animals, often via contaminated water during flooding. The bacteria enter through skin, small cuts, or mucous membranes, so avoiding potentially contaminated freshwater (and wearing shoes) is the most effective prevention.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Leptospirosis is not insect-borne, so insect repellent does not prevent it.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Handling poultry is not a described route of transmission for Leptospira.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Person-to-person transmission of Leptospira is rare, so safe sex is not a relevant preventive measure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bed nets prevent mosquito-borne disease such as lymphatic filariasis, not water-borne leptospirosis.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man returns from a camping trip during which he waded through a flooded river. He presents with sudden onset of high fever, severe myalgia especially in the calves, headache, and red eyes without discharge. Laboratory tests reveal mild jaundice and elevated liver enzymes. Which of the following infections is most consistent with this presentation?

explanation.summary: Acute febrile illness with calf myalgia, conjunctival suffusion, and jaundice after freshwater exposure points to leptospirosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Leptospirosis follows exposure to contaminated freshwater and produces fever, myalgia (especially in the calves and lower back), headache, and photophobia with conjunctival suffusion (red eyes without purulent discharge). Progression to jaundice and hepatic involvement reflects more severe disease, and L. interrogans causes the majority of clinical cases.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chikungunya is an arbovirus causing acute fever with prominent arthralgia, not calf myalgia with jaundice and freshwater exposure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Dengue is an arbovirus causing febrile illness with rash and should be considered in the differential, but it does not classically cause conjunctival suffusion with jaundice from freshwater exposure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: W. bancrofti causes chronic lymphedema rather than an acute febrile illness with jaundice.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Zika is an arbovirus causing a mild febrile illness with rash, not this waterborne hepatic picture.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man from a tropical endemic region presents with chronic swelling of his right leg and recurrent episodes of fever. On examination, the leg shows non-pitting edema and markedly thickened skin. Which of the following infections is most consistent with this clinical presentation?

explanation.summary: Chronic lymphedema with skin thickening (elephantiasis) and recurrent fevers in an endemic region is characteristic of lymphatic filariasis from W. bancrofti.
explanation.whyCorrect: Adult W. bancrofti worms live in the lymphatic vessels and, with the host immune response, cause dilation, blockage, and destruction of lymphatics. Chronic disease from repeated infection produces lymphedema and elephantiasis (thickening and fibrosis of the skin and subcutaneous tissue), often with recurrent acute inflammatory episodes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chikungunya is an arbovirus causing acute fever and arthralgia, not chronic lymphedema.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Dengue is an arbovirus causing acute febrile illness, not chronic limb swelling with thickened skin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Leptospira causes acute febrile illness with myalgia and jaundice, not chronic elephantiasis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Zika is an arbovirus causing a mild acute febrile illness, not progressive lymphatic damage.

questions.stem: A 46-year-old woman is hospitalized with severe leptospirosis complicated by jaundice and acute kidney injury. Which of the following is the drug of choice for her treatment?

explanation.summary: Doxycycline remains the drug of choice for severe, hospitalized leptospirosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Antibiotics shorten disease duration, reduce urinary shedding, and prevent complications. For hospitalized patients with severe disease, doxycycline is still the drug of choice, with penicillin or ceftriaxone as alternatives.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Azithromycin is an alternative for outpatients with mild disease, not the drug of choice for severe leptospirosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Penicillin is listed only as an alternative to doxycycline in severe disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ceftriaxone is also only an alternative to doxycycline in severe disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Diethylcarbamazine treats lymphatic filariasis (W. bancrofti), not leptospirosis.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man is diagnosed with early lymphatic filariasis caused by Wuchereria bancrofti. Which of the following medications is preferred for early treatment?

explanation.summary: Diethylcarbamazine (DEC) is the preferred medication for early treatment of W. bancrofti.
explanation.whyCorrect: DEC, a piperazine derivative, is a potent microfilaricidal and macrofilaricidal agent active against W. bancrofti and is the preferred medication for early treatment. Because early lymphatic changes may be reversible, all patients with lymphatic filariasis are treated regardless of symptoms.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Ivermectin is used, often yearly with albendazole, to manage advanced disease rather than as preferred early treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Albendazole is an alternative agent and is used for advanced cases, not the preferred early treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Doxycycline is an alternative regimen and is not typically used for early treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Praziquantel is not effective against W. bancrofti.

questions.stem: A microbiology instructor describes a spirochete that causes a waterborne febrile illness and whose curved organism resembles a question mark under electron microscopy. Which of the following morphologies best describes Leptospira spp.?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thin, tightly coiled spirochetes with hooked ends

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gram-negative curved rods with polar flagella

explanation.summary: Leptospira are thin, tightly coiled, highly motile spirochetes with hooked ends.
explanation.whyCorrect: Leptospira spp. are coiled, thin, highly motile spirochetes with hooked ends and two periplasmic flagella, giving a question mark-like appearance. Although they have an outer membrane and are considered gram-negative, they stain poorly with Gram stain and are difficult to visualize with light microscopy.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Gram-positive cocci in clusters describes staphylococci, not a spirochete.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Gram-negative curved rods with polar flagella describes organisms such as Vibrio, not Leptospira.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Gram-negative diplococci describes organisms such as Neisseria, not a coiled spirochete.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Spore-forming gram-positive rods describe organisms such as Clostridium or Bacillus, not Leptospira.

questions.stem: A physician suspects lymphatic filariasis and orders a peripheral blood smear to detect microfilariae of Wuchereria bancrofti. To maximize the yield of the smear, when should the blood sample be collected?

choices.label: B
choices.text: In the early morning while fasting

choices.label: E
choices.text: At any time of day, since timing does not matter

explanation.summary: Microfilariae of W. bancrofti are nocturnal, so blood for a smear should be collected at night (10 PM to 2 AM).
explanation.whyCorrect: The most straightforward way to identify W. bancrofti is a peripheral blood smear stained with Giemsa or hematoxylin-and-eosin. Because the microfilariae are nocturnal and enter the blood predominantly at night, collecting the sample between 10 PM and 2 AM yields better results.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Early-morning fasting collection does not coincide with the nocturnal peak of microfilariae.
whyWrongByChoice.C: At midday the microfilariae are not at their peak in the blood, lowering smear yield.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Timing sampling to a mosquito bite is not how microfilaremia is detected.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Timing does matter for the blood smear; only antigen detection can be collected any time because antigens circulate throughout the day.

questions.stem: An elderly man with leptospirosis deteriorates and develops jaundice, acute kidney injury, and hemorrhage. This severe icterohemorrhagic form of leptospirosis is best known by which of the following names?

explanation.summary: Severe leptospirosis with jaundice, kidney injury, and hemorrhage is called Weil disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Severe leptospirosis is sometimes referred to as Weil disease or icterohemorrhagic leptospirosis and is associated with children, the elderly, and patients with pre-existing conditions. Shock, hemorrhage, jaundice, and acute kidney injury are common in severe cases.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Tropical pulmonary eosinophilia is a hypersensitivity reaction seen in lymphatic filariasis, not a form of leptospirosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Elephantiasis is a chronic complication of lymphatic filariasis, not severe leptospirosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The filarial dance sign is an ultrasound finding of moving worms in filariasis, not a leptospirosis syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Conjunctival suffusion is a mild sign of leptospirosis, not the severe icterohemorrhagic form.

questions.stem: A patient in a region where dengue and chikungunya are endemic presents with an undifferentiated febrile illness, and leptospirosis is suspected. Given the difficulty of culturing the organism and the limitations of serology, which of the following is the preferred method to confirm the diagnosis?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Light microscopy of a wet mount

explanation.summary: PCR is the preferred confirmatory test for leptospirosis because culture is difficult and serology is limited.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cultures of Leptospira are time consuming and difficult and are not used for routine diagnosis, and serology has limitations. Because of these constraints, PCR (nucleic acid amplification) is the preferred method of confirming a diagnosis, and leptospirosis should always be considered in the differential in arbovirus-endemic areas.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cultures of Leptospira are time consuming and difficult and are not employed in routine diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Leptospira stain poorly with Gram stain and are difficult to visualize, so Gram stain is not confirmatory.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The thin cells are difficult to visualize under a light microscope, making wet-mount microscopy unreliable.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A single antibody titer is limited; titers take up to 2 weeks to rise and prior exposure can elevate them, so paired titers are needed rather than one acute sample.

questions.stem: A patient with lymphatic filariasis develops a cough, wheezing, and shortness of breath resembling asthma. Laboratory studies show marked eosinophilia and elevated IgE levels. Which of the following best explains this presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A hypersensitivity reaction to microfilariae in the blood

choices.label: E
choices.text: Acute kidney injury from the parasite

explanation.summary: Asthma-like symptoms with eosinophilia and elevated IgE in filariasis reflect tropical pulmonary eosinophilia, a hypersensitivity reaction to microfilariae.
explanation.whyCorrect: Some patients in the acute phase develop a hypersensitivity reaction to microfilariae in the blood known as tropical pulmonary eosinophilia (TPE). This produces asthma-like symptoms including coughing, wheezing, and shortness of breath, with laboratory findings of eosinophilia and elevated IgE.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Weil disease is a severe form of leptospirosis, not a manifestation of lymphatic filariasis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Secondary bacterial cellulitis complicates chronic lymphedema but does not cause asthma-like symptoms with eosinophilia and elevated IgE.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hydrocele is scrotal swelling from chronic lymphatic obstruction, not a pulmonary hypersensitivity syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acute kidney injury is a feature of severe leptospirosis, not this eosinophilic respiratory reaction.

questions.stem: A patient with mild leptospirosis is treated as an outpatient with doxycycline. By which of the following mechanisms does doxycycline exert its antibacterial effect?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inhibition of bacterial cell wall synthesis

explanation.summary: Doxycycline is a tetracycline that inhibits protein synthesis at the 30S ribosomal subunit.
explanation.whyCorrect: For outpatients with mild leptospirosis, treatment is similar to that for rickettsia, and doxycycline (a tetracycline and 30S protein synthesis inhibitor) is the go-to agent.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Inhibition of the 50S subunit describes macrolides such as azithromycin, the alternative agent, not doxycycline.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cell wall synthesis inhibition describes beta-lactams such as penicillin, not a tetracycline.
whyWrongByChoice.D: DNA gyrase inhibition describes fluoroquinolones, not doxycycline.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Folate synthesis inhibition describes sulfonamides, not doxycycline.

questions.stem: A medical student is reviewing how Wuchereria bancrofti is transmitted to humans. Through which of the following vectors is W. bancrofti predominantly transmitted?

explanation.summary: W. bancrofti is a nematode transmitted by the bites of female mosquitoes.
explanation.whyCorrect: W. bancrofti is a nematode (roundworm) transmitted via the bites of female mosquitoes; depending on the region it is predominantly carried by Culex, Anopheles, or Aedes spp. Mosquito control with treated bed nets, repellents, and insecticide spraying reduces transmission.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Sandflies transmit other pathogens (such as Leishmania), not W. bancrofti.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tsetse flies transmit African trypanosomes, not W. bancrofti.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Blackflies transmit Onchocerca, a different filarial nematode, not W. bancrofti.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ixodes ticks transmit spirochetes such as Borrelia, not W. bancrofti.

questions.stem: A pharmacology student is comparing chemotherapeutic drugs. She notes that many agents exert their main effect on a single phase of the cell cycle, whereas alkylating agents inhibit DNA replication regardless of the cell's activity. During which portion of the cell cycle are alkylating agents active?

explanation.summary: Alkylating agents are cell cycle independent.
explanation.whyCorrect: Alkylating and platinum agents inhibit DNA replication at all stages of the cell cycle by cross-linking DNA. Because cross-linking can occur with any DNA, they affect both cycling and resting cells rather than one specific phase.

whyWrongByChoice.A: G1 is a growth phase; alkylating agents are not restricted to it and act independently of the cell cycle.
whyWrongByChoice.B: S phase is when DNA is synthesized, but alkylating agents are not S-phase specific.
whyWrongByChoice.C: G2 is a phase-specific target for other drug classes, not for cell cycle-independent alkylating agents.
whyWrongByChoice.D: M phase (mitosis) is targeted by microtubule inhibitors, not by alkylating agents.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man undergoing cyclophosphamide chemotherapy reports that since his diagnosis he has started each day with yogurt, a banana, and a cup of grapefruit juice. His physician advises him to stop the grapefruit juice because it interferes with a process that is required for cyclophosphamide to work. Which of the following processes is affected?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alkylation of the N-7 position of guanine

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bioactivation by hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes

explanation.summary: Cyclophosphamide is a prodrug requiring CYP450 bioactivation, which grapefruit inhibits.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cyclophosphamide requires initial bioactivation into active metabolites by cytochrome P450 (CYP450) enzymes in the liver. Grapefruit juice is a potent inhibitor of CYP3A4, so it interferes with this activation step and reduces the drug's effect.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alkylation at N-7 of guanine is carried out by the active metabolites after bioactivation; it is the downstream effect, not the step grapefruit blocks.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mesna is activated in the kidney and is a chemoprotective agent, unrelated to grapefruit's CYP inhibition.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Free radical formation describes the mechanism of bleomycin, not cyclophosphamide bioactivation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Activation by hydrolyzation in the cytoplasm describes platinum agents such as cisplatin, not cyclophosphamide.

questions.stem: A 39-year-old woman with acute lymphoblastic leukemia is about to begin cyclophosphamide therapy. To reduce her risk of hemorrhagic cystitis, her oncologist orders aggressive hydration and one additional agent. Which of the following agents is most appropriate?

explanation.summary: Mesna plus hydration prevents cyclophosphamide-induced hemorrhagic cystitis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acrolein, a metabolite of nitrogen mustards, collects in the bladder and inflames the mucosal lining, causing hemorrhagic cystitis. Mesna is activated in the kidney, binds acrolein in the urine to form an inactive compound that is safely excreted, and does not interfere with cyclophosphamide's antitumor effect.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Methylene blue is used as a chemoprotective agent against ifosfamide neurotoxicity, not to prevent hemorrhagic cystitis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Leucovorin rescues cells from antimetabolite (methotrexate) toxicity and has no role in preventing acrolein-induced bladder injury.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Allopurinol prevents tumor lysis-related hyperuricemia, not hemorrhagic cystitis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dexrazoxane is cardioprotective against anthracyclines and does not protect the bladder.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with chronic myeloid leukemia has been receiving busulfan for several months. He now presents with a dry cough, progressive dyspnea, low-grade fever, and bibasilar crackles on auscultation. Which of the following adverse effects is most likely responsible for his presentation?

explanation.summary: Busulfan is classically associated with pulmonary fibrosis ("busulfan lung").
explanation.whyCorrect: Busulfan causes dose-dependent pulmonary toxicity. Cough, dyspnea, basilar crackles, and fever raise suspicion for busulfan lung (pulmonary fibrosis), which can begin as early as 6 weeks after starting treatment or up to 10 years later.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hemorrhagic cystitis is the signature bladder toxicity of cyclophosphamide, not busulfan, and presents with hematuria and dysuria.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cardiotoxicity is the hallmark of doxorubicin (an anthracycline), not busulfan.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nephrotoxicity is characteristic of platinum agents such as cisplatin, not busulfan.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CNS toxicity is associated with the nitrosourea carmustine, which crosses the blood-brain barrier.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man is diagnosed with a high-grade glioma. His oncologist selects an alkylating agent specifically because of its ability to reach the tumor site. Which of the following agents was most likely chosen?

explanation.summary: Carmustine crosses the blood-brain barrier and is used for CNS tumors.
explanation.whyCorrect: Carmustine is a nitrosourea that requires bioactivation, cross-links DNA (interstrand), and crosses the blood-brain barrier, making it useful for treating CNS tumors.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cyclophosphamide is a widely used nitrogen mustard but is not selected for its CNS penetration.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Busulfan has an affinity for myeloid cells and is used in bone marrow transplant conditioning, not primarily for CNS tumors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cisplatin is a platinum agent associated with ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity, not with preferential CNS penetration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Procarbazine is a nonclassic alkylating agent; the brick highlights carmustine, not procarbazine, for crossing the blood-brain barrier.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old man with Hodgkin lymphoma is being treated with procarbazine. He is also taking a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor for depression. He develops agitation, hyperthermia, diaphoresis, and clonus. Which property of procarbazine best explains this reaction?

explanation.summary: Procarbazine has MAO-inhibitor activity and can precipitate serotonin syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: The nonclassic alkylating agent procarbazine has monoamine-oxidase inhibitor activity, so it can cause serotonin syndrome in patients who concurrently take serotonergic drugs such as SSRIs.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Procarbazine's serotonin syndrome risk comes from MAO inhibition, not from inducing CYP450 enzymes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Free radical generation describes bleomycin's mechanism and does not cause serotonin syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.D: N-7 guanine alkylation is the DNA cross-linking mechanism of classic alkylating agents and is unrelated to serotonin toxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Platinum-based DNA damage describes cisplatin and carboplatin, which do not cause serotonin syndrome.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old woman receiving cisplatin for a solid tumor develops new tinnitus and high-frequency hearing loss, and her serum creatinine has risen since starting therapy. These findings are characteristic adverse effects of which class of chemotherapeutic agents?

explanation.summary: Platinum agents (cisplatin, carboplatin) characteristically cause ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cisplatin and carboplatin are platinum-based agents that cause direct DNA damage and intrastrand cross-linking; their signature toxicities are ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Nitrogen mustards such as cyclophosphamide are best known for hemorrhagic cystitis, not ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Alkyl sulfonates such as busulfan characteristically cause pulmonary fibrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nitrosoureas such as carmustine are associated with CNS toxicity and pulmonary toxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nonclassic alkylating agents such as procarbazine cause serotonin syndrome and a disulfiram-like reaction.

questions.stem: Researchers are studying how alkylating agents kill tumor cells. They observe that drug metabolites covalently modify DNA, after which replication halts, the cell cycle arrests, and the cell undergoes apoptosis. Which molecular event best describes the primary mechanism of these agents?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cross-linking of DNA through alkylation at the N-7 position of guanine

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stabilization of microtubules to prevent mitotic spindle breakdown

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inhibition of topoisomerase II, preventing DNA unwinding

choices.label: D
choices.text: Incorporation of a false pyrimidine analog into replicating DNA

choices.label: E
choices.text: Antagonism of folate metabolism to block nucleotide synthesis

explanation.summary: Alkylating agents alkylate the N-7 position of guanine and cross-link DNA.
explanation.whyCorrect: Drug metabolites alkylate the N-7 position of a guanosine nucleotide and can covalently attach to two separate nucleotides, cross-linking DNA. In this state DNA replication cannot continue, the cell cycle is arrested, and the cell undergoes apoptosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Microtubule stabilization describes taxanes such as paclitaxel, not alkylating agents.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Topoisomerase II inhibition describes drugs such as etoposide, not alkylating agents.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Incorporation of a false pyrimidine analog describes antimetabolites, which are cell cycle-specific, unlike alkylating agents.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Folate antagonism describes methotrexate, an antimetabolite, not an alkylating agent.

questions.stem: During a discussion of DNA repair, an instructor explains that interstrand cross-links produced by alkylating agents are more toxic to cells than intrastrand cross-links. By which repair pathway are the more toxic interstrand cross-links processed?

explanation.summary: Interstrand cross-links require homologous recombination, making them more toxic.
explanation.whyCorrect: Interstrand cross-links must be repaired by the more complicated homologous recombination pathway, which involves formation of double-stranded breaks, making repair more difficult and the process more toxic to the cell.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Nucleotide excision repair handles intrastrand cross-links, which are more easily repaired and therefore less toxic.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Base excision repair addresses small, non-bulky base lesions, not interstrand cross-links.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mismatch repair corrects replication errors such as mismatched bases, not cross-links.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Direct reversal by a methyltransferase removes simple alkyl adducts and does not resolve interstrand cross-links.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old woman receiving ifosfamide for a sarcoma becomes confused and develops encephalopathy and cerebellar signs during her infusion. Which of the following agents is used to prevent or treat this form of toxicity?

explanation.summary: Methylene blue is the chemoprotective agent for ifosfamide neurotoxicity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ifosfamide use is associated with neurotoxicity, including encephalopathy and cerebellar dysfunction. Methylene blue can be used as a chemoprotective agent to prevent this neurotoxicity.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Mesna prevents hemorrhagic cystitis by binding acrolein in the urine, not ifosfamide neurotoxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Leucovorin rescues normal cells from methotrexate (antimetabolite) toxicity, not from ifosfamide-induced encephalopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Amifostine is not the agent the brick identifies for ifosfamide neurotoxicity; methylene blue is used.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dexrazoxane protects against anthracycline cardiotoxicity, not ifosfamide neurotoxicity.

questions.stem: A 9-year-old boy with an inherited bone marrow failure syndrome is scheduled for an allogeneic bone marrow transplant. As part of the conditioning regimen, he receives an alkylating agent chosen to ablate his existing marrow and make space for donor cells. Which agent is most consistent with this use?

explanation.summary: Busulfan's marrow-ablating effect makes it a conditioning agent before transplant.
explanation.whyCorrect: Busulfan has an affinity for myeloid cells and causes profound myelosuppression. This property is used clinically in conditioning protocols, where busulfan ablates the recipient's bone marrow and generates space for transplanted donor cells.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Carmustine is used for CNS tumors because it crosses the blood-brain barrier, not for its marrow-ablating role in transplant conditioning.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cisplatin is a platinum agent used for solid tumors and is known for ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Procarbazine is a nonclassic alkylating agent associated with serotonin syndrome, not transplant conditioning.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carboplatin is a platinum agent and does not have busulfan's specific myeloablative conditioning role.

questions.stem: A 39-year-old woman two weeks into cyclophosphamide therapy notices significant hair loss and painful sores in her mouth. Which of the following best explains why these tissues are affected?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alkylating agents also kill rapidly dividing normal cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Alkylating agents are selective for tumor cells and spare all normal tissues

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cyclophosphamide deposits directly in hair and mucosal tissue

choices.label: D
choices.text: The effects result from an allergic reaction to the drug

choices.label: E
choices.text: The findings are caused by acrolein accumulation in the mucosa

explanation.summary: Alkylating agents damage rapidly dividing normal cells, causing alopecia and mucositis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Alkylating agents target all rapidly dividing cells, so they kill not only cancer cells but also normal rapidly growing cells such as hair follicle cells, oral and gastric mucosa, and bone marrow, producing alopecia and mucositis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Alkylating agents are not tumor selective; their toxicity to rapidly dividing normal cells is precisely why these effects occur.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The toxicity is due to killing rapidly dividing cells, not direct tissue deposition of the drug.
whyWrongByChoice.D: These effects reflect cytotoxicity to proliferating cells, not an allergic (hypersensitivity) reaction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acrolein accumulation causes hemorrhagic cystitis in the bladder, not hair loss or oral mucositis.

questions.stem: Antimetabolite chemotherapeutic agents such as methotrexate and 5-fluorouracil kill cancer cells by interfering with DNA replication. During which phase of the cell cycle are these agents most active?

explanation.summary: Antimetabolites act during DNA synthesis (S phase) of the cell cycle.
explanation.whyCorrect: Antimetabolites are structural decoys of the nucleoside and folate substrates used to build DNA, so they inhibit DNA synthesis, which only occurs during the S phase. This is why they are especially effective against rapidly proliferating cells.

whyWrongByChoice.A: G1 is a growth/gap phase characterized by cell growth, not active DNA synthesis, so antimetabolites are less effective here.
whyWrongByChoice.B: G2 is a gap phase for double-check repair before mitosis and does not feature the DNA synthesis these drugs target.
whyWrongByChoice.C: M phase is mitosis; microtubule inhibitors, not antimetabolites, act here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: G0 is a quiescent, non-dividing state with no DNA synthesis to disrupt.

questions.stem: A 9-year-old girl with acute lymphoblastic leukemia is treated with methotrexate. The drug prevents regeneration of the tetrahydrofolate cofactor required for thymine synthesis. Inhibition of which enzyme is directly responsible for methotrexate's mechanism of action?

explanation.summary: Methotrexate is an antifolate that inhibits dihydrofolate reductase.
explanation.whyCorrect: Methotrexate blocks dihydrofolate reductase, the enzyme that reduces dihydrofolate (DHF) to tetrahydrofolate (THF). Without regenerated THF, the methylated folate cofactor for thymine (dTMP) synthesis cannot be produced, so DNA synthesis in cancer cells halts.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Thymidylate synthase is the enzyme inhibited by 5-fluorouracil, not methotrexate.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DNA polymerase is inhibited by cytarabine, not by the antifolate methotrexate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Thymidine phosphorylase converts certain prodrugs to 5-FU within tumors and is unrelated to methotrexate's action.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ribonucleotide reductase is not the target described for methotrexate in this pathway.

questions.stem: A patient receiving high-dose methotrexate develops mucositis and a falling blood count. Leucovorin is administered to protect normal cells and reverse the toxicity. Which property of leucovorin allows it to 'rescue' cells from methotrexate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It inhibits thymidylate synthase to slow DNA turnover

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a folic acid analog that does not require activation by dihydrofolate reductase

choices.label: C
choices.text: It directly binds and chemically inactivates methotrexate in the plasma

choices.label: D
choices.text: It accelerates renal excretion of methotrexate

choices.label: E
choices.text: It stimulates bone marrow stem cells independently of folate

explanation.summary: Leucovorin bypasses the methotrexate-blocked step because it does not need dihydrofolate reductase.
explanation.whyCorrect: Leucovorin is a folic acid analog that does not require activation by dihydrofolate reductase. It therefore bypasses the pathway methotrexate blocks and restores the folate cofactor needed to form thymidine, rescuing normal cells from myelosuppression.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Leucovorin restores folate for DNA synthesis; it does not inhibit thymidylate synthase.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Leucovorin does not chemically bind or neutralize methotrexate; it works by refilling the folate pool downstream of the block.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The rescue mechanism described in the source is metabolic bypass, not enhanced renal clearance.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Leucovorin acts through the folate pathway; it does not stimulate stem cells independent of folate.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with cancer is treated with 5-fluorouracil. After conversion to its active metabolites, 5-FU forms a complex with folic acid that renders a key enzyme of thymidine synthesis dysfunctional. Which enzyme is inhibited?

explanation.summary: 5-Fluorouracil inhibits thymidylate synthase.
explanation.whyCorrect: Activated 5-FU binds folic acid and the complex binds thymidylate synthase, rendering it dysfunctional. Without thymidylate synthase, the single carbon cannot be transferred to dUMP to produce dTMP, so DNA synthesis fails.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Dihydrofolate reductase is the target of methotrexate, not 5-fluorouracil.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DNA gyrase is a bacterial enzyme and is not part of the antimetabolite mechanism described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Adenosine deaminase is not the enzyme targeted by 5-FU in thymidine synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Xanthine oxidase relates to purine breakdown and allopurinol, not to 5-FU's mechanism.

questions.stem: A 61-year-old man with indolent B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma is started on cladribine. Cladribine is a prodrug that must be phosphorylated intracellularly before it can incorporate into DNA. What is its active form?

explanation.summary: Cladribine is activated by phosphorylation to Cd-ATP.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cladribine is a purine nucleoside prodrug activated by phosphorylation to Cd-ATP. This active form incorporates into DNA, causing DNA strand breakage and shutdown of DNA synthesis and repair.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The source identifies the active form as Cd-ATP, not a monophosphate intermediate.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Adenosine triphosphate is a general cellular energy carrier, not the cladribine-specific active metabolite.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 5-Fluorouracil is a pyrimidine analog produced from other prodrugs, not the active form of cladribine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 6-Thioguanine is a separate purine analog and is not the active form of cladribine.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman taking methotrexate for rheumatoid arthritis presents with fatigue, easy bruising, and frequent infections. A CBC shows reductions in all cell lines and an elevated mean corpuscular volume with megaloblasts on marrow examination. Which adverse effect best explains these findings?

explanation.summary: Methotrexate has high affinity for rapidly dividing marrow cells, causing myelosuppression and megaloblastic anemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because methotrexate targets rapidly dividing bone marrow cells, myelosuppression is common. It can produce pancytopenia plus megaloblastic anemia (reduced RBCs with high MCV and megaloblasts). Low platelets cause bruising and low leukocytes cause infections.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cardiotoxicity is characteristic of DNA intercalators such as doxorubicin, not methotrexate.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Peripheral neuropathy is caused by microtubule inhibitors such as vincristine.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nephrotoxicity is a listed toxicity of cladribine, not the marrow findings described here for methotrexate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ototoxicity is associated with platinum-based agents such as cisplatin.

questions.stem: Certain pyrimidine analog prodrugs are converted into 5-fluorouracil preferentially within tumor tissue, which can reduce systemic adverse effects. Which enzyme, expressed at higher levels in tumors than in healthy tissue, catalyzes this conversion?

explanation.summary: Thymidine phosphorylase converts prodrugs such as cytarabine to 5-FU, with greater tumor expression.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source states that cytarabine is converted to 5-FU by thymidine phosphorylase, an enzyme with greater expression in tumors than in healthy tissues. This tumor-selective activation potentially reduces adverse effects.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Dihydrofolate reductase regenerates folate cofactors and is the target of methotrexate, not a prodrug-activating enzyme.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Thymidylate synthase is the enzyme inhibited by activated 5-FU, not the one that produces it.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Thymidine kinase is not the enzyme the source names for converting the prodrug to 5-FU.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cytidine deaminase is not identified in the source as the enzyme responsible for this conversion.

questions.stem: Leucovorin is given alongside 5-fluorouracil, but its clinical consequence is opposite to what it produces in patients on methotrexate. Which of the following best describes leucovorin's effect on 5-FU therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It reverses 5-FU toxicity by bypassing the blocked pathway

choices.label: C
choices.text: It has no meaningful interaction with 5-FU

choices.label: D
choices.text: It prevents conversion of 5-FU prodrugs in tumor tissue

choices.label: E
choices.text: It selectively shields gastrointestinal mucosa from 5-FU

explanation.summary: Leucovorin augments 5-FU, raising both potency and the risk of severe myelosuppression.
explanation.whyCorrect: In contrast to reversing methotrexate toxicity, leucovorin enhances 5-FU because 5-FU forms a more stable complex with leucovorin than with folic acid. This additive effect increases chemotherapeutic potency, and myelosuppression can be severe when the two are combined.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Reversal by pathway bypass describes leucovorin's role with methotrexate, not with 5-FU.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The source explicitly describes a meaningful, augmenting interaction between leucovorin and 5-FU.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Leucovorin does not block tumor conversion of prodrugs; it stabilizes the 5-FU inhibitory complex.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Leucovorin augments 5-FU systemically and does not selectively protect gastrointestinal mucosa.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with psoriasis managed on methotrexate asks about becoming pregnant. She is counseled to use reliable contraception because methotrexate is a pregnancy category X drug. Which fetal complication is most associated with methotrexate exposure?

explanation.summary: Methotrexate is a pregnancy category X drug that can cause neural tube defects.
explanation.whyCorrect: Methotrexate is a pregnancy category X drug because it can cause neural tube defects in the fetus. This mirrors the fact that folate deficiency during pregnancy also leads to neural tube defects, and methotrexate blocks folate metabolism.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Congenital deafness is not the fetal effect the source attributes to methotrexate.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Limb shortening (phocomelia) is classically linked to thalidomide, not methotrexate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cardiac septal defects are not the methotrexate-associated malformation described in the source.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Renal agenesis is not the fetal complication linked to methotrexate here.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man on azathioprine after kidney transplantation develops gout, and his physician is cautious about adding allopurinol. Which of the following best explains this concern?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Allopurinol inactivates azathioprine, reducing its efficacy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Allopurinol and azathioprine both cause ototoxicity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Allopurinol accelerates renal excretion of azathioprine

choices.label: E
choices.text: Azathioprine inhibits allopurinol metabolism, worsening gout

explanation.summary: Azathioprine toxicity increases when combined with allopurinol.
explanation.whyCorrect: Azathioprine (a purine analog used for autoimmune disease and anti-rejection regimens after organ transplantation) shows increased toxicity when given with allopurinol, as summarized in the antimetabolite table. Clinicians therefore use caution co-prescribing the two.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The interaction increases toxicity rather than inactivating the drug or reducing efficacy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ototoxicity is a feature of platinum agents, not of azathioprine or allopurinol here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The source describes increased toxicity, not accelerated renal clearance of azathioprine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The concern is heightened azathioprine toxicity from allopurinol, not azathioprine worsening gout.

questions.stem: In the synthesis of the DNA base thymine (dTMP), a single carbon atom is added to dUMP using thymidylate synthase and a folate-derived cofactor. Antifolate drugs are effective against cancer primarily because they:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deplete the folate cofactor needed to form dTMP

explanation.summary: Antifolates block folate one-carbon metabolism, preventing thymine (dTMP) formation and DNA synthesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Antifolates inhibit folate metabolism (one-carbon metabolism), which supplies the methylated THF cofactor needed by thymidylate synthase to convert dUMP to dTMP. Without thymine, normal DNA cannot form, and rapidly dividing cancer cells undergo apoptosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Directly cleaving DNA is not the antifolate mechanism; they act by depleting the folate cofactor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DNA cross-linking at guanine describes alkylating and platinum agents, not antifolates.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inhibiting microtubule assembly describes microtubule inhibitors such as paclitaxel and vincristine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Blocking topoisomerase describes topoisomerase inhibitors such as etoposide, not antifolates.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman receiving 5-fluorouracil develops nausea, diarrhea, mucositis, and a painful erythematous rash localized to her palms and soles. This last finding is characteristic of which adverse effect of 5-FU?

explanation.summary: 5-FU causes skin rashes and redness on the extremities, known as hand-foot syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: Common adverse effects of 5-FU include nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and mucositis, along with skin rashes and redness especially on the extremities (hands and feet), a presentation called hand-foot syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Stevens-Johnson syndrome is a distinct severe mucocutaneous reaction and is not the extremity-localized rash the source attributes to 5-FU.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pulmonary fibrosis is a listed toxicity but does not explain the palm-and-sole rash described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Neural tube defects relate to methotrexate teratogenicity, not the skin finding of 5-FU.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tumor lysis syndrome is a metabolic emergency, not the characteristic extremity rash of 5-FU.

questions.stem: A pharmacologist studies two topoisomerase inhibitors. Drug X binds an enzyme that cleaves a single strand of the DNA double helix and religates it without requiring ATP hydrolysis. Drug X is topotecan. Which enzyme does topotecan inhibit?

explanation.summary: Topotecan and irinotecan are topoisomerase I inhibitors, and topoisomerase I makes a single-strand cut without ATP.
explanation.whyCorrect: Topoisomerase I cleaves one strand of the DNA double helix to relieve supercoiling tension and religates it without ATP. Topotecan and irinotecan bind topoisomerase I and prevent the religation step. A useful mnemonic is that topoisomerase I causes a ONE-strand break.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Topoisomerase II cleaves both strands and requires ATP; it is inhibited by etoposide and teniposide, not topotecan.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DNA polymerase synthesizes new DNA and is not the target of topoisomerase inhibitors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Helicase unwinds the double helix at the replication fork but is not inhibited by topotecan.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thymidylate synthase is targeted by the antimetabolite 5-fluorouracil, not by topoisomerase inhibitors.

questions.topicName: Topoisomerase Inhibitors, Microtubule Inhibitors, and Antitumor Antibiotics

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man receiving irinotecan for metastatic colon cancer develops watery diarrhea shortly after his infusion. He also has bradycardia, excessive salivation, lacrimation, pinpoint pupils, and abdominal cramping. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment for these acute symptoms?

explanation.summary: Early irinotecan-induced diarrhea is cholinergic and is treated with atropine.
explanation.whyCorrect: Irinotecan commonly causes diarrhea, and the early or acute form produces cholinergic symptoms such as bradycardia, hypersalivation, miosis, lacrimation, and abdominal cramping. These cholinergic effects are treated with atropine.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Ondansetron targets chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting, not the cholinergic syndrome described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dexamethasone is used to premedicate against hypersensitivity or fluid retention with taxanes, not for cholinergic diarrhea.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Leucovorin rescues antimetabolite (methotrexate) toxicity and has no role here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dexrazoxane is a cardioprotective iron chelator used with anthracyclines, unrelated to this presentation.

questions.topicName: Topoisomerase Inhibitors, Microtubule Inhibitors, and Antitumor Antibiotics

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with non-Hodgkin lymphoma is scheduled to begin bleomycin as part of his regimen. Before starting therapy, the oncologist orders baseline pulmonary function testing. Which dose-limiting adverse effect is this test intended to monitor for?

explanation.summary: Pulmonary toxicity (fibrosis) is the dose-limiting adverse effect of bleomycin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bleomycin causes pulmonary toxicity that presents as pneumonitis and can progress to pulmonary fibrosis (bleomycin lung) in roughly 6 to 10 percent of patients. Because this is dose-limiting and can be irreversible, baseline pulmonary function testing establishes a reference so that early toxicity can be detected.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cardiomyopathy is the hallmark toxicity of anthracyclines such as doxorubicin, not bleomycin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hemolytic-uremic syndrome is characteristic of mitomycin C.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bleomycin is unusual in that myelosuppression is rare, so it is not the reason for pulmonary testing.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Peripheral neuropathy is typical of microtubule inhibitors, not bleomycin.

questions.topicName: Topoisomerase Inhibitors, Microtubule Inhibitors, and Antitumor Antibiotics

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with breast cancer is treated with doxorubicin. Her oncologist wants to reduce her risk of the irreversible, dose-dependent cardiotoxicity associated with this drug. Which of the following agents acts as a chemoprotective agent by interfering with iron-mediated free radical generation?

explanation.summary: Dexrazoxane prevents doxorubicin-induced cardiotoxicity by chelating iron.
explanation.whyCorrect: The main concern with anthracyclines is irreversible, dose-dependent cardiotoxicity. Dexrazoxane is a chelating agent that interferes with iron-mediated free radical generation, thereby protecting cardiac tissue against anthracycline-induced injury.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Mesna prevents cyclophosphamide/ifosfamide-induced hemorrhagic cystitis, not anthracycline cardiotoxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Leucovorin is used to rescue methotrexate toxicity and does not protect the heart.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dexamethasone is used to premedicate taxane infusions, not to prevent cardiotoxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atropine treats irinotecan-induced cholinergic diarrhea and has no cardioprotective role here.

questions.topicName: Topoisomerase Inhibitors, Microtubule Inhibitors, and Antitumor Antibiotics

questions.stem: A 49-year-old woman with ovarian cancer is treated with paclitaxel. Which of the following best describes this drug's mechanism of action on the mitotic spindle?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Binds beta-tubulin and prevents microtubule depolymerization

choices.label: B
choices.text: Binds beta-tubulin and inhibits microtubule polymerization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intercalates into double-stranded DNA to block DNA and RNA synthesis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prevents religation of topoisomerase-induced DNA breaks

choices.label: E
choices.text: Alkylates DNA and produces interstrand cross-linking

explanation.summary: Taxanes such as paclitaxel stabilize microtubules by preventing depolymerization.
explanation.whyCorrect: Taxanes bind beta-tubulin and prevent mitotic spindle depolymerization. Stabilizing the polymer causes microtubules to accumulate in a nonfunctional state, so chromosomes cannot separate during mitosis and the cell dies.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Inhibiting microtubule polymerization is the mechanism of vinca alkaloids, not taxanes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DNA intercalation describes anthracyclines such as doxorubicin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Preventing religation of DNA breaks is the action of topoisomerase inhibitors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: DNA alkylation with cross-linking describes mitomycin C.

questions.topicName: Topoisomerase Inhibitors, Microtubule Inhibitors, and Antitumor Antibiotics

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man being treated with mitomycin C presents with pale conjunctivae, fatigue, petechiae, and decreased urine output. Laboratory studies show hemolytic anemia and rising creatinine. Which adverse effect of this drug is most consistent with his presentation?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion

explanation.summary: Mitomycin C can cause hemolytic-uremic syndrome with hemolytic anemia and renal failure.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mitomycin C is an antitumor antibiotic that alkylates DNA and can cause hemolytic-uremic syndrome. Presentation with pale conjunctivae, fatigue, petechiae, and oliguria reflects the combination of hemolytic anemia and renal failure that characterizes this syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Pulmonary fibrosis is the signature toxicity of bleomycin, not mitomycin C.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cardiomyopathy is caused by anthracyclines such as doxorubicin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: SIADH with hyponatremia is a rare effect of vincristine, not mitomycin C.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Secondary leukemia is associated with high-dose etoposide, not with this hemolytic-renal picture.

questions.topicName: Topoisomerase Inhibitors, Microtubule Inhibitors, and Antitumor Antibiotics

questions.stem: A 26-year-old man with testicular cancer is treated with etoposide, which acts during the S phase or early G2 phase of the cell cycle to inhibit religation of DNA breaks. Which enzyme does etoposide inhibit?

explanation.summary: Etoposide and teniposide are topoisomerase II inhibitors.
explanation.whyCorrect: Etoposide inhibits religation of the double-strand break created by topoisomerase II during the S phase or early G2 phase of the cell cycle. A helpful mnemonic is that topoisomerase II causes a TWO-strand break. At high doses etoposide can also cause secondary leukemia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Topoisomerase I is the target of topotecan and irinotecan, which act on single-strand breaks.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dihydrofolate reductase is inhibited by the antifolate methotrexate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ribonucleotide reductase is not the target of etoposide.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Beta-tubulin is bound by microtubule inhibitors such as vinca alkaloids and taxanes, not etoposide.

questions.topicName: Topoisomerase Inhibitors, Microtubule Inhibitors, and Antitumor Antibiotics

questions.stem: A 63-year-old woman is about to receive her first dose of paclitaxel. To reduce the risk of a serious infusion-related hypersensitivity reaction, which of the following premedication regimens is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dexamethasone, diphenhydramine, and an H2 receptor antagonist

explanation.summary: Paclitaxel patients are premedicated with dexamethasone, diphenhydramine, and an H2 blocker to prevent hypersensitivity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because of serious hypersensitivity reactions with paclitaxel, patients should be premedicated with dexamethasone, diphenhydramine, and an H2 receptor antagonist. This combination blunts the allergic and histamine-mediated response to the infusion.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Atropine and loperamide address irinotecan-induced diarrhea, not taxane hypersensitivity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Leucovorin and thymidine relate to antimetabolite rescue, not taxane premedication.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dexrazoxane protects against anthracycline cardiotoxicity and would not prevent an infusion reaction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mesna and hydration prevent hemorrhagic cystitis from oxazaphosphorine alkylators, not paclitaxel reactions.

questions.topicName: Topoisomerase Inhibitors, Microtubule Inhibitors, and Antitumor Antibiotics

questions.stem: A researcher examines how topoisomerase I inhibitors such as irinotecan ultimately kill cancer cells. The drug allows single-strand breaks to accumulate in the DNA. Which of the following events most directly produces the irreversible damage that leads to cell death?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A DNA replication fork collides with a single-strand break, creating an irreversible double-strand break

choices.label: B
choices.text: The drug cross-links the two DNA strands, preventing separation

choices.label: C
choices.text: The drug stabilizes microtubules so chromosomes cannot separate

choices.label: D
choices.text: The drug intercalates into DNA and halts RNA synthesis

choices.label: E
choices.text: The drug inhibits thymidylate synthase, depleting thymine

explanation.summary: Topoisomerase I inhibitors cause single-strand breaks that become lethal double-strand breaks at the replication fork.
explanation.whyCorrect: By preventing the religation step, topoisomerase I inhibitors let single-strand breaks build up. When a DNA replication fork encounters one of these breaks, an irreversible double-strand break forms, and this irreparable damage triggers cell death.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cross-linking of DNA strands is the mechanism of mitomycin C, not topoisomerase I inhibitors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Microtubule stabilization describes taxanes and does not involve DNA breaks.
whyWrongByChoice.D: DNA intercalation describes anthracyclines.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thymidylate synthase inhibition describes the antimetabolite 5-fluorouracil.

questions.topicName: Topoisomerase Inhibitors, Microtubule Inhibitors, and Antitumor Antibiotics

questions.stem: A vinca alkaloid and a taxane are both being studied for their effects on dividing tumor cells. Despite acting through opposite effects on tubulin, both classes cause cell death by a shared downstream mechanism. Which of the following best describes that shared mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Arrest of the cell in metaphase, leading to apoptosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Arrest of the cell in S phase, preventing DNA synthesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Direct alkylation of DNA at the G1 restriction point

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inhibition of topoisomerase, blocking DNA religation

explanation.summary: Microtubule inhibitors arrest cells in metaphase, which leads to apoptosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Microtubules form the mitotic spindle needed to separate chromosomes in metaphase. Both vinca alkaloids (which block polymerization) and taxanes (which block depolymerization) render the spindle nonfunctional, arresting the cell in metaphase and triggering apoptosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: S-phase arrest reflects agents that block DNA synthesis, such as antimetabolites, not microtubule inhibitors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DNA alkylation is the action of alkylating agents and mitomycin C, not microtubule inhibitors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Topoisomerase inhibition is a distinct mechanism used by topotecan and etoposide.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Blocking protein synthesis is not how microtubule inhibitors kill cells.

questions.topicName: Topoisomerase Inhibitors, Microtubule Inhibitors, and Antitumor Antibiotics

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man with an aggressive sarcoma is treated with doxorubicin. Which of the following statements about this anthracycline is correct?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is cell-cycle specific for the M phase only

choices.label: D
choices.text: It prevents religation of topoisomerase I-induced breaks

choices.label: E
choices.text: It alkylates DNA to form interstrand cross-links

explanation.summary: Anthracyclines intercalate into DNA and are cell cycle-independent.
explanation.whyCorrect: Anthracyclines such as doxorubicin and daunorubicin intercalate into double-stranded DNA and inhibit both DNA and RNA synthesis. They are cell cycle-independent agents that also generate free radicals, causing lipid peroxidation and DNA strand injury, which lead to apoptosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Anthracyclines are cell cycle-independent, not restricted to the M phase.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inhibiting microtubule polymerization describes vinca alkaloids.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Preventing religation of topoisomerase breaks describes topoisomerase inhibitors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: DNA alkylation with cross-linking describes mitomycin C, not doxorubicin.

questions.topicName: Topoisomerase Inhibitors, Microtubule Inhibitors, and Antitumor Antibiotics

questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy with acute lymphoblastic leukemia is receiving vincristine. Laboratory studies reveal hyponatremia, and further workup shows inappropriately elevated antidiuretic hormone. Which drug-related syndrome best explains these findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion

explanation.summary: Vincristine can rarely cause SIADH, producing hyponatremia from high ADH levels.
explanation.whyCorrect: In rare cases, vincristine causes hyponatremia associated with high levels of antidiuretic hormone, consistent with the syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH). Constipation and peripheral neuropathy are its more common adverse effects.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hemolytic-uremic syndrome is caused by mitomycin C and features hemolysis and renal failure, not high ADH.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tumor lysis syndrome causes hyperkalemia, hyperuricemia, and hyperphosphatemia, not isolated ADH-driven hyponatremia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Diabetes insipidus reflects deficient or ineffective ADH with hypernatremia, the opposite of this picture.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pulmonary toxicity is a bleomycin effect and does not explain the electrolyte abnormality.

questions.topicName: Topoisomerase Inhibitors, Microtubule Inhibitors, and Antitumor Antibiotics

questions.stem: A 56-year-old woman being evaluated as a living kidney donor is found on a routine complete blood count to have a white blood cell count of 21.6 x 10^9/L with 60% lymphocytes; her hemoglobin and platelet counts are low-normal. She is asymptomatic with no palpable lymphadenopathy. A peripheral smear shows numerous mature-appearing small lymphocytes with condensed chromatin and scattered smudge cells. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: An incidental absolute lymphocytosis of mature B cells with smudge cells on smear is the classic presentation of CLL/SLL.
explanation.whyCorrect: CLL/SLL is the most common low-grade non-Hodgkin lymphoma and the most common leukemia in Western adults. It is usually discovered incidentally when a blood count reveals high numbers of mature-appearing lymphocytes (lymphocytosis), and smudge cells are a frequent smear finding.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Follicular lymphoma presents with slowly progressive lymphadenopathy and the t(14;18) translocation, not an isolated incidental blood lymphocytosis with smudge cells.
whyWrongByChoice.C: MALT lymphoma is an extranodal disease that presents with gastric symptoms such as reflux and GI bleeding, not a peripheral lymphocytosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hairy cell leukemia causes pancytopenia and splenomegaly rather than lymphocytosis, and its cells show hairy projections, not smudging.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma is an aggressive lymphoma with a rapidly enlarging mass and B-symptoms, not an asymptomatic incidental lymphocytosis.

questions.stem: A peripheral blood smear from a 68-year-old man with newly diagnosed chronic lymphocytic leukemia shows numerous mature lymphocytes plus several ruptured, smeared cellular remnants. Which of the following best explains these smudge cells?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Artifacts from fragile lymphocytes crushed during slide preparation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Apoptotic neutrophils undergoing programmed cell death

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nucleated red blood cell precursors released from marrow

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fragments of megakaryocytes in the peripheral blood

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reactive plasma cells responding to infection

explanation.summary: Smudge cells are preparation artifacts formed when fragile CLL lymphocytes are crushed during smear creation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick describes smudge cells as a frequent histologic feature of CLL that arise as artifacts from fragile lymphocytes being crushed during slide preparation. They are not specific for CLL and can be seen in other lymphoproliferative disorders with leukocytes in the blood.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Smudge cells derive from the fragile malignant lymphocytes of CLL, not from apoptotic neutrophils.
whyWrongByChoice.C: They are not nucleated red cell precursors; they represent disrupted lymphocytes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: They are not megakaryocyte fragments (which would relate to platelets), but crushed lymphocytes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: They are not reactive plasma cells; they are a mechanical artifact of smear preparation.

questions.stem: Flow cytometry on the malignant cells of a patient with chronic lymphocytic leukemia demonstrates expression of CD19, CD20, and CD22 with dim light chain restriction, along with CD23 and CD200. Which additional marker, typically considered a T-cell antigen, is characteristically expressed by these cells?

explanation.summary: CLL cells aberrantly express CD5, a marker normally associated with T cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that CLL cells express B-cell markers (CD19, CD20, CD22) with light chain restriction and also express CD5, which is typically a T-cell marker, along with CD23 and CD200.

whyWrongByChoice.B: CD10 marks germinal-center cells and is characteristic of follicular lymphoma, not CLL.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CD3 is a pan-T-cell marker and is not expressed on the malignant B cells of CLL.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CD103 is among the markers used to identify hairy cell leukemia, not CLL.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CD30 is associated with Hodgkin and anaplastic lymphomas, not CLL.

questions.stem: A patient with a several-year history of chronic lymphocytic leukemia/small lymphocytic lymphoma suddenly develops weakness, fevers, and diffuse bulky lymphadenopathy. A lymph node biopsy shows sheets of large transformed cells. This transformation is most consistent with which of the following, and what is the approximate prognosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma; average life expectancy about 8 months

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hodgkin lymphoma; average life expectancy about 10 years

choices.label: C
choices.text: Follicular lymphoma; indolent with prolonged survival

choices.label: D
choices.text: Burkitt lymphoma; highly curable with chemotherapy

choices.label: E
choices.text: MALT lymphoma; cured with antibiotic therapy

explanation.summary: Richter transformation is the feared conversion of CLL into aggressive diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, with a poor prognosis.
